issue_title: Base Exception are used, instead of subclasses
Concerns on encodings
issue_body:
I see `irc/client.py` assumes all the packets are encoded in UTF-8.

But in reality, non-UTF-8 texts are around: privmsg's are truncated by server by bytes hence sometimes broken, and some servers and channels still use their own local encodings other than UTF-8.

So I think the library should have an option for non-UTF-8 modes.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/issue/8
- Originally reported by: [puzzlet](http://bitbucket.org/puzzlet)
- Originally created at: 2012-11-21T15:46:59.817

issue_title: Automate release
get_security_bars等方法内的start,count参数意义？
issue_body:
get_security_bars等方法内的start,count参数，用数据测试了下，初步理解应该是：
start：表示从现在时点反向移动几个BAR，比如今天是9-6，start=1表示获取数据最后一天是9-5
count：表示获取BAR数量，从start确定的日期时间反向计算
对分钟线也成立。

总体来说，可以这么理解：
BAR获取数据，是从当前时间对应BAR向前推（start+count）个BAR开始，count个数量的BAR。

start参数跟大家一般理解不同，翻开get_security_bars.py看了下，是不是根据通达信发包定义的？

```
def setParams(self, category, market, code, start, count):
        if type(code) is six.text_type:
            code = code.encode("utf-8")

        self.category = category

        values = (
            0x10c,
            0x01016408,
            0x1c,
            0x1c,
            0x052d,
            market,
            code,
            category,
            1,
            start,
            count,
            0, 0, 0  # I + I +  H total 10 zero
        )

        pkg = struct.pack("<HIHHHH6sHHHHIIH", *values)
        self.send_pkg = pkg

```
不知道这样理解是否正确？
建议在后续版本的文档说明下。

issue_title: Invalid statement when no extension is loaded in Nim.
issue_body:
When no extension is selected for loading glad generates an emtpy var section, which is illegal in Nim.
`findExtensionsGL` needs a discard statement when it is empty.

```nim
# Extensions
var
  # without a single entry this is illegal

#[...]#

proc findExtensionsGL() =
  discard
```

Alternatively if you do not want to treat _no extensions_ as a special case, you can give each variable it's own var section and add a doc comment to `findExtensionsGL`.

```nim
var GLAD_GL_ARB_bindless_texture*: bool
var GL_UNSIGNED_INT64_ARB*: GLenum = GLenum(0x140F)
var GLAD_GL_ARB_direct_state_access*: bool
var GL_TEXTURE_TARGET*: GLenum = GLenum(0x1006)
var GL_QUERY_TARGET*: GLenum = GLenum(0x82EA)
var GL_TEXTURE_BINDING_CUBE_MAP_ARRAY*: GLenum = GLenum(0x900A)

#[...]#

proc findExtensionsGL() =
  ## Some doc comment to make this valid even when it is empty. But it is probably 
  ## better if you put a real meaningful doc comment here.
  GLAD_GL_ARB_bindless_texture = hasExt("GL_ARB_bindless_texture")
  GLAD_GL_ARB_direct_state_access = hasExt("GL_ARB_direct_state_access")
```

issue_title: Uncaught StopIteration exception
issue_body:
Just installed `tuna` and it doesn't seem to be working on my machine.

```
> pip install tuna
> python -m cProfile -o stat.prof myscript.py
> tuna stat.prof
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "c:\users\kevin.rose\appdata\local\continuum\anaconda2\envs\sdk-v2.2\lib\runpy.py", line 174, in _run_module_as_main
    "__main__", fname, loader, pkg_name)
  File "c:\users\kevin.rose\appdata\local\continuum\anaconda2\envs\sdk-v2.2\lib\runpy.py", line 72, in _run_code
    exec code in run_globals
  File "C:\Users\kevin.rose\AppData\Local\Continuum\Anaconda2\envs\sdk-v2.2\Scripts\tuna.exe\__main__.py", line 9, in <module>
  File "c:\users\kevin.rose\appdata\local\continuum\anaconda2\envs\sdk-v2.2\lib\site-packages\tuna\cli.py", line 13, in main
    start_server(args.infile, args.browser)
  File "c:\users\kevin.rose\appdata\local\continuum\anaconda2\envs\sdk-v2.2\lib\site-packages\tuna\main.py", line 156, in start_server
    data = read(prof_filename)
  File "c:\users\kevin.rose\appdata\local\continuum\anaconda2\envs\sdk-v2.2\lib\site-packages\tuna\main.py", line 22, in read
    return read_import_profile(filename)
  File "c:\users\kevin.rose\appdata\local\continuum\anaconda2\envs\sdk-v2.2\lib\site-packages\tuna\main.py", line 110, in read_import_profile
    line = next(import_lines)
StopIteration
```

I fixed this with a one-liner updating the `read` function of `main.py` to except not only `TunaError`, but `StopIteration` as well:

```python
def read(filename):
    _, ext = os.path.splitext(filename)
    try:
        return read_import_profile(filename)
    except (TunaError, StopIteration):
        pass

    # runtime profile
    return read_runtime_profile(filename)
```

but that was a wild shot in the dark, not sure if the solution would work elsewhere.

issue_title: Roadmap and the future of Will
issue_body:
Last Update: October 5, 2017

## A Note from Steven

> "Open source projects with no funding mechanism typically stagnate and die." 
-[GreenSock](https://greensock.com/why-license)

I read that quote a few years ago when considering using GreenSock in a front-end product, and it stuck with me since, in both Will and my other projects.  At first, the statement really bugged me - but as I looked objectively at my own long list of eventually guiltily abandoned open-source work, I realized there was a deep truth to it.

Will has gone on a similar journey - it was written with the support from a couple of my day jobs, and after those left, my time dried up, and the project started to stagnate.

That stagnation has bugged me for a long time, but I didn't really have a way to solve the problem until now.

What this document outlines is a future roadmap for Will that's both open-source and revenue-generating.  A path that can keep the codebase active, healthy, and adding new features (First up: Slack), while also paying my (and hopefully other folks') bills - and with some luck, maybe even changing the world.

I recognize that a lot of people have contributed to will, and while many of you will be thrilled by the prospect of active, sustainable development, a few folks might have the "OMG WHAT KIND OF SELL OUT BULL*** IS THIS??!?!".   To those folks, I'd like to reiterate - Will is open-source, and is going to stay that way. :) 

Now let's talk about how.

_Note_: I use the royal "we" below because it feels more natural in the long-term.  Right now it's just me, and it's awkward.  Thanks for rolling with it.


# Overall Project Structure and Goals

Will is being founded as a company, and with the core chat bot product, we'll have both the open-source library and a PaaS/SaaS service (ala Wordpress.com/.org).  The core of the company is around an idea we call Personal AI.

(Read more about the mission at [http://heywill.io/mission](http://heywill.nz/mission).)

This shift means growing the project to something much bigger than a hipchat bot, and into a write-once, run anywhere platform for chat bots, AIs, and fun new holy-crap-that's-amazing ideas.

The big goal is to provide an easy-to-build-on batteries-included platform that bridges modes of communication (HipChat, Slack, SMS, Email, Telegram, FB Messenger, etc) with services (IFTTT, Amazon AI, IBM Watson, Google APIs, etc) and built-in AI tools (NLP, ML, DL, etc).  

This broader platform serves as an OS for AI development, and has the tremendously creative working name of AIOS.

Developers can build AIOS apps (our current plugins) for will using any and all of those tools, and run them on their personal wills or distribute them to a broader audience.

Will the company will focus, like Wordpress, on running the PaaS and SaaS platforms, and a marketplace for apps.  Our goal is to keep Will development brisk, bring in talented folks across the spectrum, and keep Will available to anyone, anywhere on the planet, regardless of financial or technical access. 


# Project Roadmap

Here's the nuts and bolts of how, today, we see this rolling out.

0.9.4 - Just released, catches up almost all open PRs

1.0 - Soon, closes up existing issues, code cleanup and prep for improvements

2.0 
- Console / basic text client support, for ease of development and testing.  Initial working implementation of `IOBackend`, but not guaranteed for backwards compatibility.
 - Initial split out of the "Brain" backends - analysis, generation, execution services that can be improved and swapped out (to replace the simple regex/run-everything current approach.)
 - Slack Support, `IOBackend` documented and working.  PRs for new IOs accepted.

2.1 - API Support released, via an `APIBackend` implementation of `IOBackend`.

2.2 - IFTTT Support, `SkillsBackend` documented and working.  PRs for new Skills accepted.

2.3 - Will UI, with on-the-fly config, plugin enable/disable, and restartability.

2.5 - App specs for AIOS and `AIOSApp` class released.

2.6 - Release of first five apps. TBD, but considered: (Image me, Remind me, Groceries, News Summary, Scattegories, Stale Package Finder)

3.0 - App Marketplace released, Git library integration released, and all existing Will plugins released as AIOS apps.

3.0+ - "The root of all evil is premature optimization."  Releases after 2.0 are likely to move in a direction of improved AIOS features, more built-in AI tools, smarter and richer message processing and context, cross-app communication, and speed/reliability bumps.  We'd also like to look at the feasibility of authoring apps in both Python and JS.  

That said, this will really be determined after 2.0 is released, the launch craziness settles down, and we hear from the community and customers which directions need the most support, and what pain points folks are feeling the most.

We can't wait to have those discussions.

# Release Schedule.

A new version of Will will be released on the 1st of every month, with a 12-month guarantee for `IOBackend`, `SkillsBackend`, API and AIOS APK function stability, and a minimum 6 month deprecation warning.

# Questions and Comments

I've set up this issue to talk through this new direction, hear people's thoughts, comments, suggestions, and feedback.

I'm so excited to take will in this new, bigger direction, find consistent time and energy to keep him maintained, and grow him into something amazing together.

Thank you so much for contributing to will thus far, and I can't wait to see where we take him together!

-Steven

issue_title: Reference cycles in RestartableStrategy
issue_body:
RestartableStrategy has an _add_exception_to_history method, which stores exception objects in a list, which is a subtle way to leak objects in python.

Exceptions contain a reference to the traceback object, which has references to the objects on the stack frame. If a reference is taken to the exception object by another object already in one of the stack frames, a reference cycle is immediately created.

Here is an example, where connection is a Connection with RESTARTABLE strategy, and get it's exception history:

    >>> exception_history = connection.strategy.exception_history

Let's take one of the exceptions from the history:

    >>> exception = exception_history[0][2]

It has a traceback object with a stack frame, lets have a look at its variables:

    >>> exception.__traceback__.tb_frame.f_locals
    {'self': <ldap3.strategy.restartable.RestartableStrategy object at 0x7f9e0fb5bac8>, 'counter': 0, 'unix_socket': False, 'address': [<AddressFamily.AF_INET: 2>, <SocketKind ...

We can see that it has a reference to 'self', which is the very same RestartableStrategy object that we are inspecting, thus it is a reference cycle:

    >>> id(connection.strategy)
    140316845128392
    >>> id(exception.__traceback__.tb_frame.f_locals['self'])
    140316845128392

These leaked stack frames contain references to socket objects, causing severe fd leakage (by default, every unsuccessful connection attempt will create 30 fds that are never closed).

This blog post has a nice writeup about the general issue with taking references to exceptions in Python. 
https://cosmicpercolator.com/2016/01/13/exception-leaks-in-python-2-and-3/

~~One of the possible workarounds can be `del`eting the traceback object from the exceptions, although usually I avoid taking references to exceptions at all cost.~~ edit: you can't delete the traceback from the object.

issue_title: ldap3 does not work with pyasn1==0.2.3, at least when using NTLM authentication
issue_body:
Hi,

First, this might actually be an issue with `pyasn1` instead of `ldap3` but it do not know enough of the inner workings of `ldap3` or `pyasn1` to tell for sure. 

I have installed the latest versions of `ldap3` (2.2.1) and `pyasn1` (0.2.3), where `pyasn1` was pulled as dependency of `ldap3` by `pip`. When trying to connect to any of my LDAP servers using `ldap3.NTLM` as authentication method, I get the following traceback:

    >>> import ldap3
    >>> c = ldap3.Connection('ldap://my.ldap.server.example.com', user=r'AD\my_user', password="******", authentication=ldap3.NTLM, auto_bind=True)
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 2, in <module>
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/ldap3/core/connection.py", line 325, in __init__
        self.bind(read_server_info=True)
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/ldap3/core/connection.py", line 547, in bind
        response = self.do_ntlm_bind(controls)
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/ldap3/core/connection.py", line 1245, in do_ntlm_bind
        request = bind_operation(self.version, 'SICILY_PACKAGE_DISCOVERY', ntlm_client)
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/ldap3/operation/bind.py", line 50, in bind_operation
        request['name'] = name
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 1945, in __setitem__
        self.setComponentByName(idx, value)
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 2037, in setComponentByName
        self._componentType.getPositionByName(name), value, verifyConstraints, matchTags, matchConstraints
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 2114, in setComponentByPosition
        value = componentType.clone(value=value)
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 881, in clone
        value, tagSet, subtypeSpec, encoding, binValue, hexValue
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 833, in __init__
        base.AbstractSimpleAsn1Item.__init__(self, value, tagSet, subtypeSpec)
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/base.py", line 176, in __init__
        value = self.prettyIn(value)
      File "/tmp/test/core/.tox/linux-local/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pyasn1/type/univ.py", line 1007, in prettyIn
        return bytes(value)
    TypeError: 'NtlmClient' object is not iterable

Everything works fine, if I downgrade to `pyasn1<=0.2.2`. As far as I can tell, this might be due to some larger overhaul in how `pyasn` encodes/decodes stuff. But as I said, I do not know enough about `ldap3` and `pyasn1` internals to tell for sure, if `pyasn1` now has a bug or if the way how `pyasn1` should be used has changed in any way.

issue_title: object detectors saved with C++ API can not be loaded by Python API
issue_body:
If I train a detector using the C tool

```bash
$ /train_object_detector -tv ....
```
that generates a file `object_detector.svm`. However if I load that file with the python API it doesn't work.

```python
dlib.simple_object_detector("object_detector.svm")
```
gives `RuntimeError: Error deserializing object of type int`

Interestingly however, the following does work

```python
print dlib.test_simple_object_detector(training_xml_path, "object_detector.svm")
```

and works perfectly, giving the same precision and recall as the trained C api.

issue_title: XQuartz and Anaconda Python building and library linking errors
issue_body:
tl;dr: I fixed errors building dlib by disabling the GUI, and fixed errors linking to libmkl_rt.dylib by modifying the .so with `install_name_change`.

I'm using OSX 10.11 with XCode 8.2.1 and Anaconda. I have CUDA 8.0.55 and CuDNN 5.1.10.

```
$ brew cask install Caskroom/cask/xquartz
$ brew install boost boost-python
$ mkdir build; cd build; cmake .. ; cmake --build .
```

CUDA and CuDNN are successfully detected + built by `cmake ..`. But `cmake --build .` gives me an error pretty early on:

```
[  1%] Building CXX object dlib/CMakeFiles/dlib.dir/gui_widgets/fonts.cpp.o
In file included from /Users/kyle/dlib/dlib/gui_widgets/fonts.cpp:14:
/Users/kyle/dlib/dlib/gui_widgets/nativefont.h:313:21: error: use of undeclared identifier 'XAllocColor'
```

Instead of digging into this, I just disabled the GUI:

```
$ cmake .. -DDLIB_NO_GUI_SUPPORT:BOOL=ON
$ cmake --build .
```

And it build dlib successfully. Time to build Python support (specifying no GUI here too):

```
$ cd .. ; python setup.py install --yes DLIB_NO_GUI_SUPPORT
```

Success! But then...

```
$ python
kyle@kyle ~/D/L/dlib (master)> python
Python 2.7.12 |Anaconda 4.0.0 (x86_64)| (default, Jul  2 2016, 17:43:17) 
[GCC 4.2.1 (Based on Apple Inc. build 5658) (LLVM build 2336.11.00)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
Anaconda is brought to you by Continuum Analytics.
Please check out: http://continuum.io/thanks and https://anaconda.org
>>> import dlib
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/kyle/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dlib-19.2.99-py2.7-macosx-10.6-x86_64.egg/dlib/__init__.py", line 1, in <module>
    from .dlib import *
ImportError: dlopen(/Users/kyle/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dlib-19.2.99-py2.7-macosx-10.6-x86_64.egg/dlib/dlib.so, 2): Library not loaded: libmkl_rt.dylib
  Referenced from: /Users/kyle/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dlib-19.2.99-py2.7-macosx-10.6-x86_64.egg/dlib/dlib.so
  Reason: image not found
```

Tried to learn more about this:

```
$ otool -L ~/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dlib-19.2.99-py2.7-macosx-10.6-x86_64.egg/dlib/dlib.so | grep mkl
	libmkl_rt.dylib (compatibility version 0.0.0, current version 0.0.0)
```

To me this looks like it should have an `@rpath` at the beginning? So I do this:

```
$ install_name_change -change libmkl_rt.dylib @rpath/libmkl_rt.dylib ~/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dlib-19.2.99-py2.7-macosx-10.6-x86_64.egg/dlib/dlib.so
$ python
>> import dlib
>>
```

Success! Hopefully... maybe I just delayed the inevitable, and it was linked to another dylib? Then I go to run the Python examples:

```
$ cd ../python_examples ; python face_recognition.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "face_recognition.py", line 50, in <module>
    import dlib
ImportError: dlopen(/Users/kyle/Documents/Learning/dlib/python_examples/dlib.so, 2): Library not loaded: libmkl_rt.dylib
  Referenced from: /Users/kyle/Documents/Learning/dlib/python_examples/dlib.so
```

Looks like there's another copy of `dlib.so` here. I suppose I could just delete it, or make the same change. I opt to make the same change:

```
$ install_name_change -change libmkl_rt.dylib @rpath/libmkl_rt.dylib dlib.so
$ python face_recognition.py 
Call this program like this:
   ./face_recognition.py shape_predictor_68_face_landmarks.dat dlib_face_recognition_resnet_model_v1.dat ../examples/faces
You can download a trained facial shape predictor and recognition model from:
    http://dlib.net/files/shape_predictor_68_face_landmarks.dat.bz2
    http://dlib.net/files/dlib_face_recognition_resnet_model_v1.dat.bz2
```

Getting closer...

```
$ curl -O http://dlib.net/files/shape_predictor_68_face_landmarks.dat.bz2
$ curl -O http://dlib.net/files/dlib_face_recognition_resnet_model_v1.dat.bz2
$ bunzip2 shape_predictor_68_face_landmarks.dat.bz2
$ bunzip2 dlib_face_recognition_resnet_model_v1.dat.bz2
$ python face_recognition.py shape_predictor_68_face_landmarks.dat dlib_face_recognition_resnet_model_v1.dat ../examples/faces
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "face_recognition.py", line 74, in <module>
    win = dlib.image_window()
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'image_window'
```

Right, I didn't install the GUI components. I comment out all the lines referencing the `win` variable, and:

```
$ python face_recognition.py shape_predictor_68_face_landmarks.dat dlib_face_recognition_resnet_model_v1.dat ../examples/faces
Processing file: ../examples/faces/2007_007763.jpg
Number of faces detected: 7
Detection 0: Left: 93 Top: 194 Right: 129 Bottom: 230
-0.0512203
0.0150111
0.0642802
-0.0438789
...
```

Success!

Overall I'm posting this issue to ask a couple questions:

1. Should I have done something differently with XQuartz at the beginning?
2. Is there a variable I should set in CMake to work with Anaconda correctly? I saw in another issue you recommend just disabling Anaconda completely for building and running dlib. Is this still the recommendation, or is there another way?

`##
# things_manager project build script
##

Import('env')

# Add third party libraries
lib_env = env.Clone()
SConscript( [env.get('SRC_DIR') + '/src/system_libs.scons', env.get('SRC_DIR') + '/src/thirdparty_libs.scons'], exports = 'lib_env')

module_lib_env = lib_env.Clone()
target_os = env.get('TARGET_OS')

######################################################################
# Build flags
######################################################################
module_lib_env.AppendUnique(CPPPATH = ['inc', 'src'])

if target_os not in ['windows', 'winrt']:
	module_lib_env.AppendUnique(CXXFLAGS = ['-Wall'])
	if target_os != 'android':
		module_lib_env.AppendUnique(CXXFLAGS = ['-pthread'])

if target_os == 'android':
	module_lib_env.AppendUnique(CXXFLAGS = ['-frtti', '-fexceptions'])

######################################################################
# Source files and Targets
######################################################################
module_lib_src = env.Glob('src/*.cpp')

######################################################################
# Targets
######################################################################
module_lib_env.InstallTarget( 'libThirdPartyDlib_18_14',module_lib_src)
`

Thank you.

issue_title: Illegal Instruction(core dumped) on AWS
issue_body: Hello people, I am trying to compile the dnn version of training car detection model on AWS and later I want to train my own model. But when I run the training binary I get Illegal instruction error (see image). Has anyone used dlib on AWS and experienced this error ? Either way, any ideas as to how to debug this ? Thank you very much.

issue_title: README.md example is incorrect
issue_body:
The calc example does not work. You need to wrap "calc.g" w/ an open.  If you don't, you get:
```>>> import plyplus
>>> plyplus.Grammar("calc.g").parse("(1 + 2) * -3")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/jmg/work/scobreak/p/lib/python2.7/site-packages/plyplus/plyplus.py", line 560, in __init__
    self._grammar = self._create_grammar(grammar, source, tab_filename, options)
  File "/Users/jmg/work/scobreak/p/lib/python2.7/site-packages/plyplus/plyplus.py", line 565, in _create_grammar
    grammar_tree = grammar_parser.parse(grammar)
  File "/Users/jmg/work/scobreak/p/lib/python2.7/site-packages/plyplus/grammar_parser.py", line 156, in parse
    return _parser.parse(text, lexer=lexer, debug=debug)
  File "/Users/jmg/work/scobreak/p/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ply/yacc.py", line 333, in parse
    return self.parseopt_notrack(input, lexer, debug, tracking, tokenfunc)
  File "/Users/jmg/work/scobreak/p/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ply/yacc.py", line 1063, in parseopt_notrack
    lookahead = get_token()     # Get the next token
  File "/Users/jmg/work/scobreak/p/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ply/lex.py", line 386, in token
    newtok = self.lexerrorf(tok)
  File "/Users/jmg/work/scobreak/p/lib/python2.7/site-packages/plyplus/grammar_lexer.py", line 57, in t_error
    raise Exception("Illegal character in grammar: %r in %r" % (t.value[0], t.value[:10] ))
Exception: Illegal character in grammar: '.' in '.g'
```

if instead you do:
```>>> plyplus.Grammar(open("calc.g")).parse("(1 + 2) * -3")
start(mul(add(number(u'1'), add_symbol(u'+'), number(u'2')), mul_symbol(u'*'), neg(number(u'3'))))
```

you can see that it works.

issue_title: 建议调整Windows下的警告
pyfs keyring fails horribly on pyfs 0.5.
issue_body:
pyfs has released a new, backward-incompatible version that adds Python 3 support. Unfortunately, this new version has serious problems with keyring, probably rooted in fundamental problems in configparser.

Because configparser writes native 'str' objects to the stream (e.g. `fp.write("\n")`), those calls fail on Python 2 if the file is opened in text mode and fail on Python 3 if the file was opened in binary mode (because fs is strict about the input type even on Python 2).

I won't be able to solve this issue, so I'm going to mark the tests as xfail and hope a volunteer can help resolve the issues. If they're not resolved, I'll consider pulling support for pyfs.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/156

issue_title: Using PyJWT on App Engine
issue_body:
As suggested by the documentation PyJWT could be used in environments where `cryptography` cannot be installed (ex. App Engine).

In a local virtualenv I installed `cryptography`, `ecdsa`, `pycrypto` to test my code.

Code using the default implementation works as expected:
```
import jwt
key = "<key read from .p8 file>"
alg = 'ES256'
headers = { "alg": alg, "key": "value" }
payload = {"key": "value"}
token = jwt.encode(payload, key, algorithm=alg, headers=headers)
```

However using the suggested workaround registering the `ecdsa` algorithm and using the exact same code and key results in an error:
```
import jwt
from jwt.contrib.algorithms.py_ecdsa import ECAlgorithm

jwt.unregister_algorithm('ES256') # needs to be unregistered first for some reason
jwt.register_algorithm('ES256', ECAlgorithm(ECAlgorithm.SHA256))

key = "<key read from .p8 file>"
alg = 'ES256'
headers = { "alg": alg, "key": "value" }
payload = {"key": "value"}
token = jwt.encode(payload, key, algorithm=alg, headers=headers)
```
Error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
  File "./ENV/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jwt/api_jwt.py", line 56, in encode
    json_payload, key, algorithm, headers, json_encoder
  File ".../ENV/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jwt/api_jws.py", line 102, in encode
    key = alg_obj.prepare_key(key)
  File ".../ENV/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jwt/contrib/algorithms/py_ecdsa.py", line 44, in prepare_key
    key = ecdsa.SigningKey.from_pem(key)
  File ".../ENV/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ecdsa/keys.py", line 159, in from_pem
    privkey_pem = string[string.index(b("-----BEGIN EC PRIVATE KEY-----")):]
ValueError: substring not found
```

As I understand it `ecdsa` expects a different key format.
- so I either convert my key to the expected format (still looking into this, suggestions welcome)
- or PyJWT handles that conversion to provide a hassle-free fallback when needed

This issue might be `ecdsa` specific and considered out of scope for this project, but I would love to hear suggestions.

As a side note: I had to use `unregister_algorithm` before `register_algorithm` which wasn't mentioned in the documentation.

issue_title: Invalid syntax in setup.py
issue_body:
The commit 01d70cbaafaec1ba60a1c27454d3d3b686510a66 changedthe syntax for `python_requires`, to an invalid syntax according to [PEP 345 - version specifiers](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0345/#version-specifiers) . Basicly, the '=' operator will cause an error when setuptools runs 
```
error in bt setup command: 'python_requires' must be a string containing valid version specifiers; Invalid specifier: '=2.7'
```

because it is not acceptable as per the guidelines in PEP 345. I don't know your repository rules, but I'll submitted a pull request to fix this issue.

issue_title: Handle non-string 'Authorization' header more gracefully
issue_body:
I'm adding mohawk to a legacy web.py project, and was performing the http auth header lookup like so:

``` python
auth_header = web.ctx.environ.get('HTTP_AUTHORIZATION')
...
Receiver(lookup_hawk_credentials,
         auth_header,
         url,
         ...)
```

However if the auth header isn't present, this results in a value of `None` being passed to the Receiver constructor, which results in:

```
  File "/home/Ed/.virtualenvs/of/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mohawk/receiver.py", line 82, in __init__
    parsed_header = parse_authorization_header(request_header)
  File "/home/Ed/.virtualenvs/of/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mohawk/util.py", line 158, in parse_authorization_header
    parts = auth_header.split(',')
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'split'
```

Whilst the API docs do state that the auth header should be a string:
http://mohawk.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#receiver

...they say the same for the others that default to `None`, so it's easy to think that a `None`-value will be handled.

Instead perhaps it should raise either a generic `HawkFail` (like happens if the header doesn't start with 'hawk': [link](https://github.com/kumar303/mohawk/blob/0.3.1/mohawk/util.py#L160-L162)) or else a new exception for missing header?
http://mohawk.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#exceptions

(Using mohawk 0.3.1)

issue_title: Py3 compat: sock.recv() returns bytes, not str - TypeError in connect() in managesieve.py
issue_body:
I have looked into your mentions of Python3 compatibility, and created a virtualenv with Python 3.6.
As soon as STARTTLS is initialized, the following exception appears when reading from the socket:

```python
  File "~/.virtualenvs/sievelib/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sievelib/managesieve.py", line 489, in connect
    if not self.__get_capabilities():
  File "~/.virtualenvs/sievelib/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sievelib/managesieve.py", line 267, in __get_capabilities
    code, data, capabilities = self.__read_response()
  File "~/.virtualenvs/sievelib/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sievelib/managesieve.py", line 198, in __read_response
    line = self.__read_line()
  File "~/.virtualenvs/sievelib/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sievelib/managesieve.py", line 162, in __read_line
    self.__read_buffer += nval
TypeError: must be str, not bytes
```
This error appears, because Python 3 returns Bytes in [socket.recv()](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/socket.html#socket.socket.recv), not str as in 2.7. A concatenation with the initialized `self.__read_buffer = ""` (str) does not work.

It's easy to reproduce:
```python
>>> a = "abc"
>>> b = 'def'.encode('utf8')
>>> a += b
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: must be str, not bytes
>>> type(a), type(b)
(<class 'str'>, <class 'bytes'>)
>>> a += b.decode('utf8')
>>> a
'abcdef'
```

This can be fixed with `self.__read_buffer += nval.decode('utf8')` in managesieve.py line 162, but the TypeError then appears at another place. So I guess it's a general socket handling compatibility problem.

issue_title: No definitions found for certain scenario
issue_body:
Please see a detailed description and discussion in tkf/emacs-jedi#281, which turns out to be probably a Jedi issue. In brief:

1. Clone [this repository](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr): `git clone https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr`
2. Go to [line 49](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_imu_camera_calibration/IccCalibrator.py#L49) and try to find definitions of `addDesignVariables`. No definitions are found, yet the `addDesignVariables` function is defined in [IccSensors.py](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_imu_camera_calibration/IccSensors.py#L316), which is a python file in the same directory.

Since [line 177](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_calibrate_imu_camera#L177) calls the [registerCamChain](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_imu_camera_calibration/IccCalibrator.py#L58) function with `camChain` (an `IccCameraChain` object) argument, Jedi should make the guess that `self.CameraChain` is an `IccCameraChain` object and thus should be able to find the definition of `addDesignVariables` on [line 49](https://github.com/ethz-asl/kalibr/blob/master/aslam_offline_calibration/kalibr/python/kalibr_imu_camera_calibration/IccCalibrator.py#L49), which is a method of the `IccCameraChain` class.

issue_title: offsetof() support is incomplete
issue_body:
pycparser 2.13 added support for offsetof, and it is listed in the CHANGES file as "offsetof() the way gcc implements it". However, there are two differences between gcc's implemenation and pycparser's.

The important one: pycparser defines the syntax for `offsetof()` as 
```
        primary_expression  : OFFSETOF LPAREN type_name COMMA identifier RPAREN
```
but [gcc defines it](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc-4.9.1/gcc/Offsetof.html) as 
```
     primary:
             "__builtin_offsetof" "(" typename "," offsetof_member_designator ")"
     
     offsetof_member_designator:
               identifier
             | offsetof_member_designator "." identifier
             | offsetof_member_designator "[" expr "]"

```

The less important one (also visible above) is that gcc supports spelling `offsetof` as `__builtin_offsetof`.

issue_title: [RFC 6121] Investigate whether RosterClient has a race condition
issue_body:
Following scenario:

1. Initial roster fetch sends ``<iq/>``
2. Server responds to ``<iq/>``, but coroutine doesn’t get scheduled yet
3. Roster modification takes place and Server sends roster push ``<iq/>``
4. Roster push gets processed
5. Response to initial roster fetch gets processed

Off the top of my head we might end up with an incorrect view on the roster in this case, namely without the change from the roster push.

This boils down to the highly asynchronous processing of IQ requests and responses in fact, because if we were processing these synchronously, the order of stanzas would get us enough guarantees.

Ideally, it would be great to have a guarantee that send() returns before handlers for any stanza received after the ``<iq/>`` result are invoked. We might actually have that with some asyncio loops (those which guarantee that multiple callbacks scheduled with call_soon are executed in the order of their scheduling), but I’m not sure that is guaranteed to be true for all loops anywhere.

issue_title: Bot doesn't execute function or exits with code 0
issue_body:
To summarize, when a bot admin uses !update command, it starts the update chain.

1. Gets a member list and server roles
2. Sees if any of the members already have any region role, removes them from the list if they do (to avoid making unnecessary queries)
3. Checks if there's someone with their nick/name in the regions, then returns a dict. With a key/value of nick/name : list_of_regions
4. If a dict is returned (the members list could be empty), it starts setting roles 
5. Finds the nick/name in the dictionary and adds role to the member with the respective nick/name.

However, it stops working just after entering step 4. Sometimes it exits the bot with exit code 0, others the bot keeps running, but without reaching doing anything in step 4.

The functions for step 4 and 5:
```python
async def roles(user, future_roles, server_roles):
    """
    :param user - A Member that is going to be assigned a role
    :param future_roles - A list of Names that match existing Roles.names
    :param server_roles - Self explanatory
    """
    print("you have reached roles function")
    for role in server_roles:
        if role.name in future_roles:
            await  client.add_roles(user, role)
            print("Role {} added to {}".format(role.name, user.display_name))


async def set_roles(user_region_dict, list_users, server_roles):
    """
    :param user_region_dict - A dictionary with Keys being Member's nicks or names, values the regions where they play
    :param list_users: - Self explanatory
    :param server_roles - Self explanatory

    How it works:
                Checks each member.nick/member.name in the dictionary and assigns the respective member
                that is in list_users due to the index variable.

                It calls the function roles with future_roles param being the region(s) where they play
    """
    print("You've reached set roles")
    for index, member in enumerate(list_users):
        print("You reached set roles step 2")
        if member.display_name in user_region_dict:
            print("Setting roles to {}".format(member.display_name))
            await roles(list_users[index], user_region_dict[member.display_name], server_roles)
```

Creating the issue because I'm not sure if it's my code that is wrong or if it's conflicting with the discord.py library.
I'm somewhat familiar with python but I probably make a lot of mistakes so feel free to call out on anything that I could improve ^^
[Picture to exemplify a test run](http://i.imgur.com/obv5NAr.png?1)
Note: The "Setting roles in a bit" is before entering step 4

issue_title: KafkaConsumer.poll() can not read all messages.
issue_body:
I create a topic with one partition in Kafka using the following command.

```shell
bin/kafka-topics.sh --create --zookeeper localhost:2181 --topic test --replication-factor 1 --partitions 1 --config max.message.bytes=2147483647
```

After that, I send 128 messages by KafkaProducer.send().

```python
def produce_msg(servers, topic, msg):
    producer = kafka.KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers=servers, acks=1, buffer_memory=2147483647, max_block_ms=6000000, max_request_size=2147483647)
    producer.send(topic, msg)
    producer.flush()
for msg in messages:
    produce_msg('localhost:9092', 'test', msg)
```

About 5 minutes later, Kafka receives all messages. And I check its logs to confirm.
Then, I use KafkaConsumer.poll() to read messages for many times. But each time I can only get about fourty or fifty messages, not all of 128 messages.
 
```python
def consume_msg(servers, topic):
    consumer = kafka.KafkaConsumer(bootstrap_servers=servers, group_id=None, auto_offset_reset='earliest', max_partition_fetch_bytes=2147483647)
    consumer.subscribe(topic)
    data = consumer.poll(timeout_ms=6000000)
    partition = kafka.TopicPartition(topic=topic, partition=0)
    records = data.get(partition)
    messages = [i.value for i in records]
    print 'kafka consumer receives messages with a number of %s' % len(messages)
    return messages
consume_msg('localhost:9092', 'test')
```

However, if I use the following codes to read messages, I can get all 128 messages.
So, how to correctly use KafkaConsumer.poll() to read all messages?

```python
def consume_msg(servers, topic):
    consumer = kafka.KafkaConsumer(bootstrap_servers=servers, group_id=None, auto_offset_reset='earliest', max_partition_fetch_bytes=2147483647)
    consumer.subscribe([topic])
    valid = 0
    for msg in consumer:
        if len(msg.value) > 0:
            valid += 1
            print valid
consume_msg('localhost:9092', 'test')
```

issue_title: Pivot Table support
issue_body:
[Pivot Tables](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pivot_table) is a data summarization tool found in data visualization programs such as spreadsheets or business intelligence software. Among other functions, a pivot table can automatically sort, count total or give the average of the data stored in one table or spreadsheet, displaying the results in a second table showing the summarized data. Pivot tables are also useful for quickly creating unweighted cross tabulations. The user sets up and changes the summary's structure by dragging and dropping fields graphically. This "rotation" or pivoting of the summary table gives the concept its name.

I have found a pretty nice library online that does this, called [PivotTable.js](http://nicolas.kruchten.com/pivottable/examples/index.html) but it doesn't let you do anything with the table once you have selected your columns and rows. You cant sort the data.

PivotTable support something they call Renderers. These essentially allow users to replace the table view with a custom function, Their examples show that the user can swap between Graph Types and Tables with ease. It seems unnecessary to rewrite the whole thing so maybe it would be easier if you guys supplied a Renderer to allow us to sort the data once its displayed.

Are there any chances we could see something like this in Tablesorter?

issue_title: Large coordinates are not returned exactly, because of conversion to floats
issue_body:
I've noticed that the coordinates of the paths returned by `pyclipper.Execute()` are represented using floats. This introduces some inaccuracies for very large coordinate values as not all values of an `int64_t` can be represented by a `double`. I've added a test case here showing the problem:

https://github.com/Feuermurmel/pyclipper/tree/inexact-coordinates

I see that the library supports automatic scaling of coordinate values by setting the global `pyclipper.SCALING_FACTOR`. This of course means that, unless this variable is set to 1, a type other than `int` is necessary to represent coordinates. Thus I'm not sure what the best way is to solve this problem. I currently see the following options:

– Remove `pyclipper.SCALING_FACTOR`, but there are probably some people using it.
– Only use floats when `pyclipper.SCALING_FACTOR` is not set to 1. Seems a bit hack-ish.
– Add a second implementation of `pyclipper.Pyclipper` which ignores the scaling factor and uses ints instead. This seems obscure and bloats the library.

issue_title: Close twice on handler cause it hangs forever
issue_body:
I noticed if the handler `close` gets called twice, like this

```Python
import logging
import os
import sys


sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), '..')))  # noqa
from watchtower import CloudWatchLogHandler

handler = CloudWatchLogHandler(stream_name='run_logging')
logger = logging.getLogger('run_logging')
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
logger.addHandler(handler)
logger.info('msg')
handler.close()
```

The first time by us, the second time by `logging` module `atexit` registered function, it will hang the program forever, as the thread was already closed, the `exit` message will never get processed.

issue_title: Wait for event:volumeChanged before leaving "pending" state in volume control
issue_body:

if __name__ == '__main__':

issue_title: Use proper API in http_to_file
issue_body: http_to_file() in twitter_utils.py gets data out of the Response object as Response.raw.data which is a property from the underlying urllib3 library that doesn't do the proper decoding.  If the server sent gzipped data it won't be decompressed. Response.content should be used instead as it handles the decoding for you (see https://github.com/requests/requests/blob/master/requests/models.py#L724 , decode_content=True)

issue_title: Some of the data sanitization applied in write() should be usable when reading
issue_body:
pretty_midi.py write() function does a lot of nice sanity checking and normalization of the data. For example, it sorts note onsets by time, pitch and velocity. It also checks for note-on and note-off ordering. And some other nice stuff (default time signature, etc). 

Much of this sanitization should be available without having to write the file and read it back in. It would mean pulling a bunch of code out of write() into a sanitize_instruments() function.  It would have to be slightly rewritten to operate over instruments rather than tracks.  I guess we'd want it to be non-destructive so that it would be optional to use at the time of reading in.

issue_title: Issues with High DPI support in latest CEF on Windows
issue_body:
High DPI was last tested by me in the v31 release. There were some changes in upstream CEF in regards to High DPI support. For example in phpdesktop it was no more required to change zoom level according to OS DPI settings.

SetProcessDpiAware code was commented out in phpdesktop:
https://github.com/cztomczak/phpdesktop/blob/c00988f69348b73b6dee27bdf45d145b719e2a3d/phpdesktop-chrome39/dpi_aware.cpp#L14

CEF Python has the same code. It needs to be checked whether this needs to be commented out as well:
https://github.com/cztomczak/cefpython/blob/824d7ce1efacde0a9f0e453cd69325d23fe3e7a9/src/client_handler/dpi_aware.cpp#L163

In the wxpython.py example you can see this code that enables High DPI support:
```
if WINDOWS:
        settings["auto_zooming"] = "system_dpi"  # High DPI support
        # noinspection PyUnresolvedReferences, PyArgumentList
        cef.DpiAware.SetProcessDpiAware()  # Alternative is to embed manifest
```

PHP Desktop embeds DPI aware manifest by default:
https://github.com/cztomczak/phpdesktop/blob/master/phpdesktop-chrome57/DeclareDPIAware.manifest

issue_title: Invalid types for window handles on Windows 64-bit
issue_body: In cefpython's code there are multiple places where type "long" is used to keep window handles. This is wrong, because on Windows 64-bit the long type is 32-bit. Window handles and other pointer types should be defined as `uintptr_t` (from libc.stdlib). Also replace any "long long" types with correct pointer type.

issue_title: Cancelling during `merge` iteration emits waring
issue_body:
Consider this code:

    async def main():
        async def values(value):
            while True:
                await asyncio.sleep(0.2)
                yield value

        async def task():
            async for value in merge.raw(values("a")):
                print(value)

        try:
            await asyncio.wait_for(task(), 0.5)
        except asyncio.TimeoutError:
            pass

    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main())

I get the following output:

    a
    a
    Task was destroyed but it is pending!
    task: <Task pending coro=<async_generator_asend()> wait_for=<Future pending cb=[<TaskWakeupMethWrapper object at 0x7f2324a9f168>()]> cb=[_wait.<locals>._on_completion() at /usr/lib64/python3.6/asyncio/tasks.py:380]>

this can be fixed by adding a `try`/`except` in [merge](https://github.com/vxgmichel/aiostream/blob/master/aiostream/stream/combine.py#L80):

    streamers = {}
    try:
        # ...
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        for task in streamers:
            task.cancel()
        raise

It probably happens at other places where `asyncio.ensure_future` is used, I'll have a look.

issue_title: Test issue
issue_body:
# Opening XlsxWriter Issues

## Asking questions about using XlsxWriter

General questions on how to do something with the XlsxWriter module should be
asked on [StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/xlsxwriter).
Add the ``xlsxwriter`` tag to the question. This has a better chance of
getting several answers and also helps others who might have similar questions
in the future.


See below for information on adding a Bug Report or Feature Request.


## Reporting Bugs

Modify the following to suit.


Title: Issue with SOMETHING

Hi,

I am using XlsxWriter to do SOMETHING but it appears to do SOMETHING ELSE.

I am using Python version X.Y.Z and XlsxWriter x.y.z and Excel version X.

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem:

```python

import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('hello.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.write('A1', 'Hello world')

workbook.close()

```

Notes:

1. Ensure that the example code can be run to generate a file that
   demonstrates the issue.
2. Only include code that relates to the issue.
3. Remove non-relevant text from this template.
4. If you are seeing an issue in LibreOffice, OpenOffice or another third
   party application, also test the output with a version of Excel.
5. You can get the required version numbers as follows:

        python --version
        python -c 'import xlsxwriter; print(xlsxwriter.__version__)'


## Feature requests

Add `Feature request:` to the title.

In the comment section describe the feature that you would like to be added.

If you are currently using a workaround you can show that workaround.

issue_title: Issue with blank strings in write_rich_string()
issue_body:
If one of the string parts of a rich string is blank the resulting Excel file will raise a warning "We found a problem with some content in 'file.xlsx'...".

```
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('rich_strings.xlsx')
my_work_sheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

my_work_sheet.set_column('A:A', 50)

format_1 = workbook.add_format({'color': 'red'})
format_2 = workbook.add_format({'color': 'blue'})


my_work_sheet.write_rich_string(0, 0, 
                                format_1, "Some text in format 1 ", 
                                format_2, "", 
                                format_1, "Again in format 1")

workbook.close()
```

Originally reported on [StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/43199359/python-xlsxwriter-repaired-records-string-properties-from-xl-sharedstrings-xml/43248203#43248203).

issue_title: Cache file behavior does not distinguishing on error
issue_body: Hi, When trying to monitor the clustercron state by checking /tmp/clustercron_cache.json I found it does not distinguish an error state. Master state: {"isodate": "2016-08-29T11:22:59.026312", "master": true} Slave state: {"isodate": "2016-08-29T11:22:59.026312", "master": false} Error state: {"isodate": "2016-08-29T11:22:59.026312", "master": false} In this case I triggered an error by polling a non existing ELB (a use-case we encountered after an ELB change had taken place). I think it should remove cache file or have an extra "master" value, error. Benefit of the second one is that you won't bombard the API if you have lots of high frequency cron jobs, but the first one might be a valid option too is some cases. Maybe make the option between the two configurable. This was only one error state example, there are of course more. So maybe you want more error states in the cache file. Nice thing is that in that case you can monitor for and catch errors happening every so often. issue_body:
I'm trying to crawl a PGN file an ran into an interesting issue: empty games are silently ignored! This was a bit surprising when adding the games to a database (sqlite3) because I was told the game already existed :)

Here's a clean file:
```[Event "freechess.org"]
[Site "-"]
[Date "2017.4.14"]
[Round "?"]
[White "Krakonkas"]
[Black "GuestMPZQ (Player)"]
[Result "*"]
[PlyCount "0"]



[Event "freechess.org"]
[Site "-"]
[Date "2017.4.16"]
[Round "?"]
[White "GuestWVVL (Player)"]
[Black "GuestTNMT"]
[Result "1-0"]
[PlyCount "32"]

1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Nxe5 Nxe4 4.Nxf7 Kxf7 5.Qf3+ Nf6 6.Bc4+ Ke8 7.O-O d6 8.d3
Nc6 9.Bg5 Be7 10.Bxf6 Ne5 11.Bb5+ c6 12.Qh5+ g6 13.Qg5 Nf7 14.Re1 Nxg5 15.Bxe7
Kf7 16.Bxd8 c5 1-0
```

So doing something like this
```
In [12]: pgn=open('clean.pgn')

In [13]: g=chess.pgn.read_game(pgn)

In [14]: g
Out[14]: <chess.pgn.Game at 0x7efcb6b891d0>

In [15]: print(g)
[Event "freechess.org"]
[Site "-"]
[Date "2017.4.16"]
[Round "?"]
[White "GuestWVVL (Player)"]
[Black "GuestTNMT"]
[Result "1-0"]
[PlyCount "32"]

1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nf6 3. Nxe5 Nxe4 4. Nxf7 Kxf7 5. Qf3+ Nf6 6. Bc4+ Ke8 7. O-O d6 8. d3 Nc6 9. Bg5 Be7 10. Bxf6 Ne5 11. Bb5+ c6 12. Qh5+ g6 13. Qg5 Nf7 14. Re1 Nxg5 15. Bxe7 Kf7 16. Bxd8 c5 1-0
```

So when going through what `scan_offsets()` gives me, I will effectively get the same game object twice. If I could get some sort of notification that `read_game` skipped an incomplete/invalid game, the code could just continue with the next offset. If not, I will not be able to easily tell if I'm really trying to add an existing game or just that I got one (or more) invalid games in between. Any suggestions?

Here are some possible usage scenarios of having a charging switch within HA:

issue_title: ObjectProxy objects are not copyable
issue_body:
I was trying to use ObjectProxy in a context where the object in question sometimes gets copied by `copy.copy`, e.g.:

```python
from wrapt import ObjectProxy
import copy
print(copy.copy(ObjectProxy(1)))
```

which raises an exception:

```
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-29-184d66a435a2> in <module>()
----> 1 print(copy.copy(ObjectProxy(1)))

/Users/ryan/.pyenv/versions/3.5.2/lib/python3.5/copy.py in copy(x)
    103                 raise Error("un(shallow)copyable object of type %s" % cls)
    104
--> 105     return _reconstruct(x, rv, 0)
    106
    107

/Users/ryan/.pyenv/versions/3.5.2/lib/python3.5/copy.py in _reconstruct(x, info, deep, memo)
    296         if deep:
    297             state = deepcopy(state, memo)
--> 298         if hasattr(y, '__setstate__'):
    299             y.__setstate__(state)
    300         else:

ValueError: wrapper has not been initialized
```

To fix this, I implemented the __copy__ and __deepcopy__ methods in a subclass of ObjectProxy:

```python
from wrapt import ObjectProxy
import copy

class CopyableObjectProxy(ObjectProxy):

    def __copy__(self):
        copied_wrapped = copy.copy(self.__wrapped__)
        return self.__class__(copied_wrapped)

    def __deepcopy__(self):
        copied_wrapped = copy.deepcopy(self.__wrapped__)
        return self.__class__(copied_wrapped)

x = 1
xp = ObjectProxy(x)
xcp = CopyableObjectProxy(x)

print(copy.copy(x))
try:
    print(copy.copy(xp))
except ValueError:
    print("Copy of xp failed")
print(copy.copy(xcp))
```

However, this presumably will not work for more complex proxy classes that have their own state that also needs copying. Is there a way to implement this generally for any proxy object, or does every subclass of ObjectProxy need to provide a custom __copy__() method if it wants to be copyable?

issue_title: Refactor to support latest changes distributed in PyPI
issue_body:
Need some slight tweaks to allow a release including the `pip`-friendly changes I tried to incorporate into what was `0.9`. (See Issue #121 and PR #120)

- [ ]  add wheel support to setuptools-related files (e.g. setup.py, setup.cfg)
- [ ]  make sure project works equally on Py2 and Py3 (required for universal wheels)

Ideally this refactor allows people that clone the project to simply run `pip install -r requirements-dev.txt` and they have everything required to run tests and hack on the code. Requirements files tend to do a better job making repeatable installations (see https://packaging.python.org/requirements/)

issue_title: use flask-profiler in wsgi production environment
issue_body:
Hi, for my flask application https://github.com/zimmerst/DmpWorkflow, i started using your brilliant profiler -- first off, thanks a lot for doing all the hard work for me. But I seem to be unable to use the flask profiler in the wsgi-production environment but only when i initialize the application by hand typing "python core/manage.py runserver"

For reference, here's the relevant code piece that includes the profiler:
```python
from DmpWorkflow.config.defaults import cfg
import flask_profiler
from DmpWorkflow import version
from socket import getfqdn
kind = cfg.get("global", "installation")

if kind == 'server':    
    from flask import Flask
    from flask.ext.mongoengine import MongoEngine
    app = Flask(__name__)
    app.config.update(LOGGER_NAME="core")
    app.config['MONGODB_DB'] = cfg.get("database", "name")
    app.config['MONGODB_USERNAME'] = cfg.get("database", "user")
    app.config['MONGODB_PASSWORD'] = cfg.get("database", "password")
    app.config['MONGODB_HOST'] = cfg.get("database", "host")
    app.config['MONGODB_PORT'] = int(cfg.get("database", "port"))
    # make connection numbers unbound
    app.config['MONGODB_MAXPOOLSIZE'] = None 
    # time out after 100ms
    app.config['MONGODB_WAITQUEUETIMEOUTMS'] = 100
    app.config["DEBUG"] = True
    app.config["flask_profiler"] = {
        "enabled": app.config["DEBUG"],
        "storage": {
            "engine": "sqlite",
            "FILE": "/tmp/flask-profiler.sql"
        },
        "basicAuth":{
            "enabled": True,
            "username": "myuser",
            "password": "mypassword"
        }
    }

    db = MongoEngine(app)
    
    def register_blueprints(app):
        # Prevents circular imports
        from DmpWorkflow.core.views import jobs
        from DmpWorkflow.core.admin import admin
        app.register_blueprint(jobs)
        app.register_blueprint(admin)
    
        if app.config['flask_profiler']['enabled']: app.logger.info("started flask profiler")
    register_blueprints(app)
    flask_profiler.init_app(app)
    
    def main():
        app.logger.info("started DmpWorkflow Server Version: %s on %s",version,getfqdn())
        app.run()
else:
    def main():
        pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    if kind == 'server':
        main()

issue_title: TypeError: replace_refs_factory() missing 1 required positional argument: 'target'
issue_body:
The above error with pandoc-fignos was reported at [greenelab/deep-review](https://github.com/greenelab/deep-review/pull/774#issuecomment-357519950):

**The problem:** There is an API incompatibility between pandoc-fignos 1.0.0 and pandoc-xnos 0.15 (the library for the pandoc-xnos/eqnos/tablenos filters).

**The solution:** Upgrade to the most recent version of pandoc-fignos.

I would like to prevent such errors from occurring in the future.  One way forward is to move pandoc-xnos to MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH versioning and require a particular MAJOR.MINOR version for a given pandoc-fignos release.  The MINOR number would be incremented for API changes.

Feedback is welcome.

issue_title: Consider including `roundtrip` in response
issue_body:
### Versions

```
OS:       
Python:   
krakenex: 
```

### What are you trying to achieve?

Have a value to check for query round-trip time.

``` python
# code sample
def now():
     return decimal.Decimal(time.time())

before = now()
response = self.query_private('AddOrder', {...}
after = now()
print('# roundtrip', after - before, 'seconds')
```

### What do you expect to happen?

Works OK as above, but could be included in decoded JSON response, since the "successful" Kraken response is a `result` key (not top-level).

The latter is also stored in `krakenex.API.response`, so a `krakenex.API.roundtrip` (or similar) could be stored for "failed" queries.

We need to be able to write python dicts to parquet. What are the chances that you'll have time to work on this? I.e. a writer class. 

My team is totally new to parquet so we have a lot to learn. We did see https://github.com/jcrobak/parquet-python/pull/13 which claims to have a writer functionality but that PR is out-of-sync and tries to solve a couple of other things at the same time. 

Would appreciate your thoughts on this project's near future. 

cc @adngdb

The MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES setting is deprecated in Django 1.10 and the MIDDLEWARE setting takes precedence. Since you've set MIDDLEWARE, the value of MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES is ignored.

And then I changed all `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES` to `MIDDLEWARE` I got the following error:

```

issue_title: Support sleeping to throttle API requests
issue_body: Have some optional parameters to the Client class to record the last time of a read or write API call and sleep until the next one is ready.  Right now I can work around it with my own bookkeeping but it seems like the pygsheets library is the correct place to handle this.

issue_title: Get_col + returnas=cell only returns first row
issue_body:
If you use get_col and set return to cell.  All you get back is the first row.

Given a spreadsheet that looks like this:
A   B
1   6
2   7
3   8
4   9
5   10

This code:

```python
import pygsheets
gc = pygsheets.authorize()
sh = gc.open('my new ssheet')
wks = sh.sheet1
col_a = wks.get_col(1, returnas='cell')
col_b = wks.get_col(2, returnas='cell')
print(col_a)
print(col_b)
print(col_a[0].value)
print(col_b[0].value)
```

Returns:

> [<Cell R1C1 '1'>]
> [<Cell R1C2 '6'>]
> 1
> 6

I recreated the get_col function using get_values, but omitted the return index ([0])

And this way i can get all the rows:

```python
col_a = wks.get_values(start=(1,1), end=(wks.rows,1),returnas='cell',majdim='COLUMNS')
col_b = wks.get_values(start=(1,2), end=(wks.rows,2),returnas='cell',majdim='COLUMNS')

print(col_a)
print(col_b)
```

returns:
> [[<Cell R1C1 '1'>], [<Cell R1C2 '2'>], [<Cell R1C3 '3'>], [<Cell R1C4 '4'>], [<Cell R1C5 '5'>]]
> [[<Cell R1C2 '6'>], [<Cell R1C3 '7'>], [<Cell R1C4 '8'>], [<Cell R1C5 '9'>], [<Cell R1C6 '5'>]]


But i don't get a list of cells, but instead a list of of lists of length 1, containing 1 cell.
And the labels are wrong (but not the values...)

```python
print("Col A")
for cell in col_a:
    print("Label: {}, cell: {}".format(cell[0].label, cell[0].value))

print("Col B")
for cell in col_b:
    print("Label: {}, cell: {}".format(cell[0].label, cell[0].value))
```

returns
> Col A
> Label: A1, cell: 1
> Label: B1, cell: 2
> Label: C1, cell: 3
> Label: D1, cell: 4
> Label: E1, cell: 5
> Col B
> Label: B1, cell: 6
> Label: C1, cell: 7
> Label: D1, cell: 8
> Label: E1, cell: 9
> Label: F1, cell: 5

issue_title: is_ratelimited increment=True does not seem to work
concat() removes audio
issue_body:
I have two mkv files and I'm trying to concatenate them. Running the following:

    in1 = ffmpeg.input('1.mkv')
    in2 = ffmpeg.input('2.mkv')
    ffmpeg.concat(in1, in2).output('out.mp4').run()

results in the correct video but with no audio information (ffmpeg reports audio=0kB even).

Running this:

    ffmpeg.input('1.mkv').output('out.mp4').run()

successfully converts 1.mkv to an mp4 file while preserving the audio

issue_title: stSort and stPersist not working if stSort contains expression + Solution
issue_body:
Hi,
sorry if this was already posted here. There are so many posts similar to my problem that I didn't know what to do. However, I also give a solution.

I found out that stSort does not correctly read the table state if the sort attribute is a method instead of property. I'm using stPersist to jsonfy the tablestate.

e.g.
```javascript
<th st-sort="sort.aMethod"..>
```
where sort is a scope object with methods for sorting.

https://github.com/lorenzofox3/Smart-Table/blob/master/dist/smart-table.js
In Line 373 (in stSort) there is 
```javascript
if (newValue.predicate !== predicate) {
```
where
```javascript
var predicate = attr.stSort; 
```
in our example it is "sort.amethod".

However, if methods are used for sorting, the ctrl.tablestate().sort.predicate contains a function binding and not a string. So the if decision in L373 cannot work since nowhere in code the function tableState.sort.predicate is created and JSON.parse does not work with functions, of course. 
(As a side note: the functionName attribute, if a binding is a function, will be empty if the sorter object looks like this:
```javascript
$scope.sorter = {
   aMethod : function () { return ... };
}
```
functionName uses prototype Function.name which will use the name between "function" and "()". However it is empty in most examples I've seen. On the other hand "name" of Function prototype doesn't seem to be readily available everywhere since it is a ES6 standard. See:
https://developer.mozilla.org/de/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Function/name
)

**SOLUTION**
I have created a plunk to show the problems
http://plnkr.co/edit/t5lzFugHPFICZCZL2W27

In the header you can find stSort with direct property name (PropertyPersistSinceName) of a row and also a method name of a sorter object in scope (EvalNotPersist). 
Click on EvalNotPersist to sort and stop then run the plunk again to see that persistance is not given.

It works, however, for the suggested solution. I created a stSortx directive (mostly copying stSortx) and added a //NEW comment to highlight the changes. In short, I also save the plain attribute content as a string, save it to a new property of the tablestate and compare it later to check whether the directive is currently responsible for sorting.
It does not need a function name and is simple imho.


I hope it helps.

Otherwise good work! :+1:

issue_title: C bindings for calculations
issue_body:
I've been working on a rather large project: getting a look-up table of sorts created in a pandas DataFrame. Essentially, what I'm doing is getting a list of 8000 color values along with an index number for each. Those get plugged into a dataframe where index and column values are the same. The  deltae for each color to every other color is calculated, then inserted into the dataframe at the appropriate index. This is done in a multiprocessing context, so I've got 4 processes running to reduce the time needed to do the  64 million deltae calculations (ideally, I should be able to cut this in half, as 1:2 will be the same as 2:1, I just haven't worked out that logic yet. )

What is important: even if it were 32 million calculations, that takes a lot of time.  I'm wondering if creating an experimental branch of colormath, which uses c bindings for the calculations is worthwhile. In a lazy way, using numba's jit decorator might work. I'm curious to see what other types of cases people are using this module for to get a handle if such an improvement would help the library as a whole.

issue_title: Can assh generate full assh binary path in ~/.ssh/config?
issue_body:
I'm using SourceTree to manage my git repos which push and pull through SSH. 
After every config YAML changes, I re-generate with `assh build > ~/.ssh/config`. 
The last line `ProxyCommand` start with **assh** instead of **the full assh binary path**, and that cause SourceTree failed to find assh when doing network operations. I have to manually append the full prefix every time I re-genrate the config. I understand that it is caused by the alias, because SourceTree internally use ssh by default, but what if my GitLab machine is behind some relay node and want to access through assh?
Also, this problem occurs when user choose a different install location instead of Homebrew's `/usr/local/bin`, and this may also brick SourceTree or any other apps. 
So I suggest that we could add a field which named like **BIN_PATH** to YAML config, and generate the full assh binary path instead of the barely assh one?

Give me a thumbs-up if you face the same pain :)

issue_title: Rejecting updates without a version
issue_body:
I haven't looked into the internals to see if this is difficult or even possible - but I'd like to start a discussion on the subject.

I'd like to be able to raise an exception if a versioned instance is modified without supplying a version value. This would close a loophole where there is some method or API call that I've forgotten to update to handle concurrency checks.

Our use case is a large complex codebase where we want to quickly add baseline protection against concurrent updates  - a strict 'fail fast' in any situation where there is a potential for conflicts.

TypeError: 'numpy.float64' object cannot be interpreted as an index
issue_body:
When I tried to invoke the stHarmonic function in the audioFeatureExtraction.py file to get pitch features, error occurs as below:
File "/disk2/pwj/pyAudioAnalysis/audioFeatureExtraction.py", line 152, in stHarmonic
    Gamma = numpy.zeros((M), dtype=numpy.float64)
TypeError: 'numpy.float64' object cannot be interpreted as an index 

After Googled it, I find this link useful:https://github.com/rbgirshick/py-faster-rcnn/issues/481
In short, the latest version of numpy has removed the ability to use floating point indices.

And I add astype(numpy.int) at the end of the line:
136     M = numpy.round(0.016 * fs).astype(numpy.int) - 1

It works fine for me. I guess there are some other places will cause this problem, if what I have done 
is reasonable, I would like to make a PR.

issue_title: Key error
issue_body:
Get a summoner with special char results a Key Error...

```py
from riotwatcher import RiotWatcher, BRAZIL
w = RiotWatcher(key='<key>', default_region=BRAZIL)
me = w.get_summoner(name='faucheurdecrân')
# KeyError: 'faucheurdecr\xc3\xa2n'
```

So, I recommend to use this strategy in RiotWatcher#get_summoner method:
```py
return self.get_summoners(names=[name, ], region=region).itervalues().next()
```
instead of 
```py
return self.get_summoners(names=[name, ], region=region)[name]
```
Final method:
```py
    def get_summoner(self, name=None, _id=None, region=None):
        if (name is None) != (_id is None):
            if name is not None:
                # name = self.sanitized_name(name)  redundant statement ... it is called in RiotWatcher#get_summoners method
                return self.get_summoners(names=[name, ], region=region).itervalues().next()
            else:
                return self.get_summoners(ids=[_id, ], region=region)[str(_id)]
        return None
```

issue_title: KaplanMeierFitter.plot() ignores `flat` argument
issue_body:
I ran into this accidentally while trying to recreate the stepped-style plot elsewhere and found that the value passed for `flat` seemed to be ignored, and the resulting plot is always stepped/flat.

In case it was related to the data I was using, I recreated it with the political regimes dataset from `lifelines.datasets.load_dd()`. Below are snippets of the plot command used for each resulting graph in the image.

```python
data = lifelines.datasets.load_dd()
kmf = lifelines.KaplanMeierFitter()
kmf.fit(data['duration'], data['observed'])
kmf.plot(ci_show=False, show_censors=True, flat=False, title='Flat False')  # title lengthened later
kmf.plot(ci_show=False, show_censors=True, flat=True, title='Flat True')  # title lengthened later
```

I dug into it a bit myself, but don't see that `plt.step()` is even ever called; in fact `matplotlib.pyplot.step()` defaults its `where` parameter to `'pre'` and I had to explicitly call `step(..., where='post')` to get it to match the default results from `kmf.plot()`. My only guess is whether it's related to calling `fill_between()` like an unintended side effect or something. 

![screen shot 2016-09-23 at 6 09 37 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1156198/18802923/eb88cc6e-81b9-11e6-8357-c9e647935b00.png)

issue_title: Unable to train a car to traverse a track
issue_body:
Car specs - 
I've modeled the car using unity and interfaced it with python using Unity ML Agents(https://github.com/Unity-Technologies/ml-agents). The car has 5 radar sensors mounted on the front bumper each of which calculates distance to nearest obstacle. The car has 2 controls, Accelerate (with range [-1, 1]), and steer (with range [-1, 1]). I've set the configuration file for 5 inputs and 3 outputs and set the activation to tanh. 

Fitness function - 
The fitness for a genome is calculated by running the car in the track and recording a distance metric based on acceleration values. (More detail about how fitness is calculated at the end)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Problem -
During training what happens is, the car learns not to go backward, but does not learn to avoid obstacle or traverse the path. The fitness values of all genomes is always from a fixed set of float values. After enough iterations all the genomes tend to have the same fitness values and it goes on without stopping.

I couldn't get what the problem is, is it a problem with modelling of the car or is it that the configuration parameters are sub standard.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

How fitness is calculated detailed - 
Consider 5 time steps of environment with acceleration values at each as [a1, a2, a3, a4, a5], for all -1 <= ai <= 1, and initial velocity, v0 = 0.
First time step - 
Distance covered in first time step             s1 = v0 * t + (1/2) * a1 * t ^2 = a1 / 2           [t = 1 time step]
Velocity at the end of first time step          v1 = v0 + a1 * t = a1 * 1 = a1             

Distance covered in second time step       s2 = v1 * t + (1/2) * a2 * t ^2 = v1 + a2 / 2 = a1 + a2/2
Velocity at the end of second time step    v2 = v1 + a2 * t = v1 + a2 * 1 = v1 + a2 = a1 + a2

Distance covered in third time step       s3 = v2 * t + (1/2) * a3 * t ^2 = (a1 + a2) * t + (1/2) * a3 * t^2
                                                                    = (a1 + a2) + (1/2) * a3 = a1 + a2 + a3/2
Velocity at the end of third time step    v3 = v2 + a3 * t = a1 + a2 + a3
... so on for all time steps
Used the following equations of motion 
s = u*t + (1/2)*a*t^2
v = u + a*t

issue_title: Capturing job exceptions with e.g. Sentry
issue_body:
I use Sentry ( https://getsentry.com/welcome/ ) to track exceptions, but chroniker handles exceptions from jobs itself before they reach the Sentry/raven code that sends them

I'm wondering how best to fix this:

1. Wrap the code of every management command in try/except and explicitly call Sentry in except - ugly!

2. Monkey patch Job.handle_run - also ugly!

3. Have an option to not handle the exceptions, probably per job - ugly, if not impossible due to the clean up code needed if the job fails

4. Have chroniker run the job through the shell - that way the exception would be in it's own process and not effect the chroniker process, but probably ugly and creates more problems

5. Have chroniker detect if sentry is installed, and pass the exception to it - I think this is the best solution, and could be extended to support other systems

Do you agree, and if so, how would you prefer it be implemented? Create a handle_exception() function in utils to pass errors to, with a function for each exception handling software to check if it's installed and pass exceptions to it?

issue_title: Swagger doesn't support converters with optional arguments
issue_body:
If one defines a route with optional arguments, http://werkzeug.pocoo.org/docs/0.11/routing/#rule-format, i.e., `@api.route('/my-resource/<string(length=2):id>')`, Swagger raises a `ValueError` (swagger.py#L82) because in senses the type converter is unsupported, i.e.,

```
from flask import Flask
from flask_restplus import Api, Resource


app = Flask(__name__)
api = Api(app)


@api.route('/my-resource/<string(length=2):id>')
class MyResource(Resource):
    def get(self, id):
        return id

    @api.response(403, 'Not Authorized')
    def post(self, id):
        api.abort(403)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(debug=True)
```

The workaround seems to be to explicitly register the converter via, 

```
from werkzeug.routing import UnicodeConverter
app.url_map.converters['string(length=2)'] = UnicodeConverter
```

which seems somewhat kludgy, especially when one has multiple signatures which use arguments.

issue_title: Token Sidejacking prevention
issue_body:
Hi mate,

I've been reading this attack vector from the OWASP: https://www.owasp.org/index.php/JSON_Web_Token_(JWT)_Cheat_Sheet_for_Java#Token_sidejacking

And I was wondering what you reckon about the option of adding "user context" and then validating it. It would be trivial to add this data as you describe here: https://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/add_custom_data_claims.html, but I'm not sure how easy would be to then add that check to the JWT validation process as well, as opposed to having to do it manually for every request an API receives if there is no native support from this module.

Cheers!

issue_title: inquiry regarding the `refreshing` of tokens
issue_body:
Hi there,

I am glad I encountered this open source, it has met all my basic needs in `protecting` my backend endpoints. Thanks to all the examples in the documentation!

I have a question though, I don't know if this is because I don't have enough knowledge on JWTokens but I am curious on how the exp field works.

Lets say a user logged in and got issued a jwt that expires in 3 seconds.
If the user tries to access the endpoint after 3 seconds has past, it is considered an old token and the user is required to log in again. This part I understand.

What happens if the user makes consequent requests within the 3 seconds. Does the exp field get updated automatically? If not, does the client have to take care of the `refreshing` of the token implicitly?

Thanks once again for the great open source!

issue_title: Question about private claims
issue_body:
I am supporting a back end server which only consumes JWT (thus, I can not influence the creation of the JWTs). The problem is that the JWT that the server receives contains some private claims like this:
```
{
  "exp": 1534854259,
  "identity": "test",
  "hello": "test",
  "foo": [
    "bar",
    "baz"
  ]
}
```

However, I saw in the documentation that the flask-jwt-extended library supports private claims (called user claims), but they are placed under the `user_claims` field, e.g.:
```
{
  "exp": 1534854259,
  "identity": "test",
  "user_claims": {
    "hello": "test",
    "foo": [
      "bar",
      "baz"
    ]
  }
}
```

I also saw that there is a `JWT_USER_CLAIMS` config option that can alter the `user_claims` string.

My question is if it is possible to get private claims that exist in the root of the object, like the `hello` in the first example.

issue_title: use a config option to disable GET/SET on TTL
issue_body:
stubbing the ticket, I can handle this.

If redis is configured to act as an LRU cache with server-side expire policies, there is no need to set the EXPIRE/SETEX or even use any TTL data.  

Having an option to disable this would:

* cut down on wire traffic
* cut down on memory used by redis (the expire data would not need to be maintained)
