issue_title: Embracing asyncio / Python >= 3.5?
issue_body: Since it's 2017, with Ray being a new distributed computing framework without a lot of legacy baggage, I am curious if there is a particular reason to maintain Python 2.7 support, which you could use better and faster concurrency primitives in Python 3.5 and up. The Python data community is rapidly distancing itself from Python 2 with Python 2.7 end-of-life in 2020, so I don't think you'd be giving up much.

issue_title: [rllib] Is A3C algorithm using async_optimizer implemented correctly as the algorithm stated in original paper?
issue_body:
### Describe the problem
<!-- Describe the problem clearly here. -->
In the [async_optimizer](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/rllib/optimizers/async_optimizer.py), it uses a list to store all the tasks, and waits for the first element in the list and then processes it. Finally, it appends a new element to the end of the list and then repeats the loop. In this implementation, if somehow the first element in the list is processing really slowly, and many other elements have finished their tasks, then they all need to wait for the first one. Actually, this implementaion is not the same as stated in ray's official documentation [here](https://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/example-a3c.html), which uses `ray.wait(gradient_list)`. The async_optimizer does not use` ray.wait() `is because the `ray.wait()` does not work because of [this issue](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/1128).

However, in the [original paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1602.01783) by google, each worker updates the policy without waiting for anything. 

Why don't we use the same strategy as the one in [async_parameter_server](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/examples/parameter_server/async_parameter_server.py). In this strategy, every worker does its own job without waiting for anybody.

Here is the pseudocode to change the original async_optimizer:
```
def _init(self):
    self.global_iteration = 0 

@ray.remote
def _help_step(self, evaluator_index):
    e = self.remote_evaluators[evaluator_index]
    while self.global_iteration < self.grads_per_step:
        e.set_weights.remote(self.local_evaluator.get_weights())
        fut = e.compute_gradients.remote(e.sample.remote())
        gradient, _ = ray.get(fut)
        self.local_evaluator.apply_gradients(gradient)
        self.global_iteration += 1

def step(self):
    workers = [_help_step.remote(i) for i in len(self.remote_evaluators)]
```

If we use this strategy, we can finish the whole training by calling step method once.

issue_title: Parsing output from datetime.isoformat
issue_body:
It seems dateparser cannot handle the default output from isoformat that includes the microseconds:

```
In [14]: dateparser.parse(datetime.utcnow().isoformat())  # output: None

In [15]: dateparser.parse(datetime.utcnow().isoformat()[:-1])

In [16]: dateparser.parse(datetime.utcnow().isoformat()[:-2])

In [17]: dateparser.parse(datetime.utcnow().isoformat()[:-3])

In [18]: dateparser.parse(datetime.utcnow().isoformat()[:-4])
Out[18]: datetime.datetime(2020, 9, 9, 2, 49, 4)

In [19]: dateparser.parse(datetime.utcnow().isoformat()[:-5])

In [20]: dateparser.parse(datetime.utcnow().isoformat()[:-6])
Out[20]: datetime.datetime(2016, 9, 20, 2, 49, 11)
```

The correct output is the last one.

issue_title: Recommended setting, minimal search
issue_body:
I have been using `chrono` for a long time for date parsing, as it was reliable and effective to my needs, but I decided to drop it as it doesn't support Python3.

Moving from `chrono` to `dateparser` was not trivial. I couldn't find any mention that adding `languages=['en']`, a common use case, would be 50 times faster than without it, I think this should be a recommended setting. Using this setting it is now almost as fast as chrono, which is ok for my needs. (I need to try to parse millions of small strings)

I also tried your `search_dates`, a great method, that can replace my own implementation of trying to parse every substring from the sentence, however, I found that it does not give the minimal result.
For example, "he retired on September 1st, 1969." would find a date "on September 1st, 1969" and not just "September 1st, 1969". Is there a way to add a minimal setting?

Thanks for this amazing repo!

issue_title: Is Subnets.reset() really necessary?
issue_body:
I recently ran a profiler on 100 frames of my data and was pretty surprised by the results. (Ignore the calls to `sleep()` — this was part of a distributed pipeline.) The real surprise was at the top of the list:

```
         98038653 function calls (97815743 primitive calls) in 274.135 seconds

   Ordered by: internal time

   ncalls  tottime  percall  cumtime  percall filename:lineno(function)
       99   41.146    0.416   41.146    0.416 subnet.py:348(reset)
  3891096   34.281    0.000   37.733    0.000 utils.py:109(__init__)
     3362   18.792    0.006   18.792    0.006 {time.sleep}
       99   17.508    0.177   59.864    0.605 subnet.py:358(compute)
  3924791   16.811    0.000   16.811    0.000 linking.py:291(_sort_key_spl_dpl)
3933169/3933069   14.686    0.000   14.745    0.000 {numpy.core.multiarray.array}
       99   13.097    0.132   39.570    0.400 linking.py:500(assign_links)
     2846   12.157    0.004   28.983    0.010 {sorted}
      100   11.128    0.111   48.861    0.489 utils.py:26(points_from_arr)
       99    8.674    0.088   41.550    0.420 linking.py:518(apply_links)
       99    7.599    0.077   12.469    0.126 predict.py:323(predict)
  3974174    6.210    0.000    6.964    0.000 subnet.py:238(assign_subnet)

       99    4.622    0.047   19.023    0.192 subnet.py:50(predict)
  3939181    4.478    0.000    5.814    0.000 {method 'sort' of 'list' objects}
       99    4.129    0.042    4.142    0.042 {method 'query' of 'scipy.spatial.ckdtree.cKDTree' objects}
  3722887    3.535    0.000   17.065    0.000 linking.py:263(adaptive_link_wrap)
```

`Subnets.reset()` is working very hard to initialize a ton of dicts, lists, and sets. But it is duplicating some work already done by `Point.__init__`. Is this an opportunity for efficiency, notwithstanding any cases (as in `find_link`?) that make this more complicated?

issue_title: Fix adding ForeignKey instance to inline model
Automatic extras on nested inlines causes field is required error
issue_body:
After playing around a bit, I noticed that if I create a top level inline and it has nested inlines, if the nested inlines have an extras value > 0, the extra inlines that are automatically there won't save the first time. All of their fields will be cleared and the "Field Is Required" error will show for all of the fields that are required on those extra inlines.

I was able to work around this by simply setting each inline to use have 0 extras and then just using the "add <inline>" buttons to add more. If my explanation wasn't clear enough, I can post a video of what is happening.

issue_title: Improper conversion of integer values for file format .xls (in2csv command)
issue_body:
I ran into some issues regarding how integer values are being treated when converting from .xls to .csv.
The docs say:


I'm not sure what the excel and csv specs say on this, whether they shed any light on how this case should be handled.

>If an XLS looks identical to an XLSX when viewed in Excel, they may not be identical as CSV. 
>For example, XLSX has an integer type, but XLS doesn’t. Numbers that look like integers from an XLS
> will have decimals in CSV, but those from an XLSX won’t.


Regardless of whether this is a real bug or not, for the sake of usability, this needs to be changed. For the average user I believe that result below is not desired. Even if the default can't be to convert "xls non integers" to integer I propose that there should at least be a flag to override this default.

Original file :
```
Number|Words|Decimal
1234|N/A|0
4|TE/ST|33.8
```
Command run:
```in2csv test1.xls | csvformat -D '|' > test1.csv```

File output:
```
-- test.xls
Number|Words|Decimal
1234.0||0.0
4.0|TE/ST|33.8
```

issue_title: Spark master IP retrieved from wrong node
issue_body: The `__init__` method of a service is not necessarily called from the same node as the service is actually run on. Because of this, the master IP of the spark cluster can't be determined in the `__init__` method. This manifests itself in connection failures once the service is running.

issue_title: Unable to advertise VPN prefixes
randSample selection in convConn and divConn not ideal for small networks
issue_body:
The [selection of a random sample](https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/netpyne/network.py#L834) from the pre/post population using:

    randSample = list(sim.unique([int(round(self.rand.uniform(0,len(preCellsTags)-1))) for i in range(4*convergence)])) # generate 4x and find unique list (to avoid duplicates)

can lead to errors for a small network/convergence size, as for e.g a population of 3 and a conv of 2 will not necessarily yield a list long enough for randSample[convergence] here

     preCellsSample[:] = [randSample[convergence] if x==postCellGid else x for x in preCellsSample] # remove post gid 

to exist. Also, there's no check that randSample[convergence] != postCellGid.

Same for divConn. There's also a finite chance for it happening for larger networks too.

Just a thought -- maybe instead of framework.py, you could use relative imports in __init__.py to achieve the same result? Then instead of e.g.

```
from . import sim
from . import analysis

from .cell import Cell, PointNeuron, Pop 
from . import utils
from . import default

import pylab
```

issue_title: Process assignment events without consuming messages
issue_body:
Description
===========
I have a peculiar consumer group setup in which I need to **process** the assign/revoke **callbacks without consuming messages** necessarily.

In my application there are messages generated by different producers that belong to their respected priority group. There are huge bursts of new messages and the consumers have limited resources, so it is not possible to process all messages in real time. It would be enough to have the higher priority messages block the lower tiers until there are none left. To achieve this priority message routing, I make consumers for each priority group (topic) and read the messages from the highest priority consumer possible.

My problem arises when there are mulitple of these router processes. Each of them knows how much messages the consumers can receive in total in a minute, but they must know how many messages they need to produce **individually**. Here's a simple equation:
`number_of_messages_current_router_process_should_produce = number_of_messages_consumers_can_process_in_total * (partitions_assigned_to_current_router_process / total_number_of_partitions_of_router_processes)`

Since the number of partitions for the given topics are mostly static and there is no metadata fetching for it (yet) in this kafka python library, I get that from configuration which is mostly fine.

I have set up the consumers with on_assign and on_revoke callbacks to keep track of how many partitions I have, but those only update when poll() is called. However, since I do not want to consume messages from the lower priority consumers those will not update their assignment for extended periods of time. Overall the router processes end up producing half of what they should because the assignment of the lower priority consumers do not get processed. (even though they are actually assigned in the background)

How to reproduce
================
There is not much to be reproduced as the documentation clearly states that callbacks are processed during poll(). There is a new consume() method which also processes events but passing num_messages=0 to it will make it do nothing.

I've looked around a little in librdkafka and found that rd_kafka_poll() could do just what I want, but confluent-kafka-python sets up the consumers with rd_kafka_poll_set_consumer() that will redirect the main kafka events to the consumer's queue. In this setup the rd_kafka_poll() is not usable according to the documentation.

Even if another setup would be used the event list for rd_kafka_poll() does not include assignment events in the documentation. I'm guessing that could be because those events are tied to the consumer all the time, but I'm not sure.

In the end my question is:
**Is there a way or could there be a way to process the assignment events of a consumer without consuming any messages?**

Checklist
=========

 - [ ] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`): ('0.11.4', 721920), ('0.11.4', 722175)
 - [ ] Apache Kafka broker version: 0.11.0.0
 - [ ] Operating system: Linux 4.16.13-2-ARCH SMP PREEMPT Fri Jun 1 18:46:11 UTC 2018 x86_64 GNU/Linux

issue_title: maximum number of items available is wrong when multiple stockrecords for the same product exist
issue_body:
I have a product with two different stockrecords (A and B). 
Stockrecord A has 2 products available (net_stock_available=2)
Stockrecord B has 15 products available (net_stock_available=15)

When a customer adds the product to his basket Stockrecord A gets selected. 
Then the customer clicks on view basket and increases the number of this products in his basket from 1 to 5.
Oscar then throws an error with the message "only two products available", although there is a stockrecord with 15 free products. 

In that case the stockrecord of the line in the basket should automatically change to the stockrecord that has sufficient capacity for the order.

issue_title: Stateless Huffman
issue_body: As discussed with @Lukasa the Huffman encoder / decoder  is currently initialized using two static structures defined in huffman_constants.py.  We could potentially remove the need for this pseudo-stateful nature and instead have a completely stateless Huffman encoder / decoder with the addition of another static structure related to decoding (encoding is currently trivial to make stateless).  This may also offer a minor performance enhancement.

issue_title: Which is the best folder to place a new file containing a custom data structure implementation?
issue_body:
Hello, everyone.
I'm a PhD student at medical image processing and I'm to excited to contribute to skimage.
I intend to implement some new graph-based techniques for image processing and analysis, then I'm gonna to implement (in Cython) a priority queue optimized for image processing.

**Which is the best folder to place a new file containing a data structure?**

issue_title: Figure of edge modes example is too small
issue_body: in http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/plot_edge_modes.html, all 5 subplots are on a single row, resulting in tiny windows. A 2-line plot would be better.

issue_title: `feature.local_binary_pattern` fails on empty array sliced from non-contiguous source
issue_body:
## Description
This is a bug somewhere, because `local_binary_pattern` has [np.ascontiguousarray](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/feature/texture.py#L295) inside.
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "sss.py", line 12, in <module>
    lbp = feature.local_binary_pattern(x, 8, 1, 'uniform')
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/skimage/feature/texture.py", line 296, in local_binary_pattern
    output = _local_binary_pattern(image, P, R, methods[method.lower()])
  File "skimage/feature/_texture.pyx", line 84, in skimage.feature._texture._local_binary_pattern (skimage/feature/_texture.c:2505)
ValueError: Buffer not C contiguous.
```

## Way to reproduce
```
import numpy as np
from skimage import feature


z = np.random.random(200**2).reshape((200, 200))
# Works
# x = z[30:10, 10:30]
# Fails
x = z[10:30, 30:10]
print(x.shape, x.size)

lbp = feature.local_binary_pattern(x, 8, 1, 'uniform')
```

- [x] Operating system and version: `Linux 4.5.0-1-ARCH x86_64 GNU/Linux`
- [x] Python version: `Python 2.7.11, Python 3.5.1`
- [x] scikit-image version (run `skimage.__version__`): `0.12.3`

issue_title: make a test runner for all tests
issue_body: Ensure all tests in `pathos.tests` can run with `nosetests` or `pytest`, or at least with a `run_all` that can be run with `python -m pathos.tests` or something like that.

`<ScalarVariable

issue_title: Django 2.0
issue_body:
Hey,

I want to discuss our strategy for `Django 2.0`. Django 2.0 only supports Python 3, so I would propose that we should have 2 branches.

- master for Version 1.0 with only Python3 support
- v0.9 with Python2 support

What are your opinion?

issue_title: Not connecting to Celery signals on Python 2 with v0.4
issue_body:
Since upgrading from v0.3.16 to v0.4.0, handlers are not connecting to Celery's signals - no stats are getting sent from Celery.

## With v0.3.16

Relevant packages:

```
celery==4.1.0
Django==1.8.19
django-statsd-mozilla==0.3.16
```

Testing via iPython:

```
In [1]: from celery import signals

In [2]: signals.task_sent.has_listeners()
Out[2]: True

In [3]: signals.task_prerun.has_listeners()
Out[3]: True

In [4]: signals.task_postrun.has_listeners()
Out[4]: True

In [5]: signals.task_failure.has_listeners()
Out[5]: True

In [6]: signals.task_sent.receivers
Out[6]: 
[((139822539469840, 9392928),
  <weakref at 0x7f2af8d29cb0; to 'function' at 0x7f2af8cbf410 (on_task_sent)>)]
```

... which is correctly connected to `celery.on_task_sent`: https://github.com/django-statsd/django-statsd/blob/cddc6dc93d7497ab92240bd42983307758a6f195/django_statsd/celery.py#L8-L13

## With v0.4.0

Relevant packages:

```
celery==4.1.0
Django==1.8.19
django-statsd-mozilla==0.4.0
```

iPython:

```
In [1]: from celery import signals

In [2]: signals.task_sent.has_listeners()
Out[2]: False

In [3]: signals.task_prerun.has_listeners()
Out[3]: False

In [4]: signals.task_postrun.has_listeners()
Out[4]: False

In [5]: signals.task_failure.has_listeners()
Out[5]: False
```

issue_title: Add license upload API in storops
issue_body:
here is the sample from Unity guide:

```
<!DOCTYPE HTML PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.01 Transitional//EN">
<html>
 <body>
   <form enctype="multipart/form-data" method="post" action="https://<IP_address>/upload/license">
     <input type="file" name="filename>"/> 
      <input type="submit"/> 
    </form>
 </body>
</html>
```
A successful upload request returns 200 OK HTTP status code. If the request does not succeed, the server returns a 4nn or 5nn HTTP status code in the response header and a message entity in the response body.

issue_title: Unity: Check OE version before attaching snap
issue_body:
Attaching snap to host only supports from Unity OE version 4.1.

Although before the version 4.1, attaching snap to host would succeed, the IO to the snap will fail.
We need to add version check to only allow attaching snap to host in version 4.1 or later.

issue_title: Remove lxml from dependency list.
issue_body:
The dependency of lxml is causing various installation problems for storops on both Windows and Linux platform.  While in fact we only utilize it for xml parsing for VNX file which is almost not used.
Remove this dependency and replace it with native python xml library to remove this installation trouble.

issue_title: Run Python lint via a git commit hook (not just TravisCI)
issue_body:
I've recently had to tweak a couple of pull requests failing the TravisCI run lint check.

My development flow would have been far smoother if the Python lint check was (also) run locally via a git commit hook.

issue_title: openaddr-process needs source-aware log streams
issue_body:
When processing a source fails, we'd like to present the debug logs from the program to the user on the dashboard at http://data.openaddresses.io/. So our code needs a way to collect per-source logs. openaddr-process is managing the jobs, using Python threads to run things in parallel. So really we need a per-Thread way to sort out logs.

Mike and I talked and we think the way to do this is to install a special `logging.Handler` in the Thread manager in `jobs.py`. It will intercept logging messages from the entire Python process and then identify which source the message came from based on the thread ID or thread name in the `LogRecord`. It can then use its own `logger.Formatter` to write them to a per-source output file. This apparatus runs alongside any other debug logs, we think all the code can be isolated in `jobs.py`.

Other ideas we discussed: having every `Logging.debug()` call include a keyword, generating new logger objects for each source, leveraging Thread-local variables somehow. The Java library log4j's [Thread Context](http://logging.apache.org/log4j/2.x/manual/thread-context.html) abstraction would be useful, but the Python library doesn't have anything like that. But we think the `LogRecord`'s thread data will work best.

PS: `logging` is [explicitly designed](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#thread-safety) to be thread-safe. We don't use `multiprocessing`, but if we do it's more complicated [how it interacts with logging](https://docs.python.org/2/library/multiprocessing.html?highlight=multiprocessing#logging).

issue_title: Caches on docket report do not get cleared
issue_body:
This is a fun corner case we haven't run into! If we do something like this:

    report = DocketReport(court='foo')
    for query_param_dict in query_parameter_dicts:
        report.query()
        report.data

We'll have a big issue. The problem is that the first time we use `report.data`, it will parse out the content and then cache it, which is fine. But the next time we use it, it will query PACER (incurring a fee), make a new tree of the HTML from PACER, and then just use all the old data.

A better way to handle this is to clear the caches any time a parse operation is run.

issue_title: Allow writing a resolver that returns a SQLAlchemy as a relay connection
issue_body:
I would like to return a relay connection for queries. Using standard graphene-sqlalchemy you can do this:

```python
class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    node = relay.Node.Field()

    all_users = SQLAlchemyConnectionField(User)
```

For various places where you need a more custom search the simplest approach is a custom resolver:

```python
class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    node = relay.Node.Field()

    all_users = graphene.List(User)

    def resolve_all_users(self, info, **args):
        # Grab the root SQLAlchemy query
        query = User.get_query(info)
        return query.filter(
            User.active,
            # and possibly a whole bunch of other filters)
        ).options(joinedload(User.emails).all()
```

This works, but it returns a plain list of users without any support for pagination or other relay connection tricks handled by SQLAlchemyConnectionField. What I am looking for is a way to return a query, and have it automatically handled in a similar way as SQLAlchemyConnectionField does. Perhaps by introducing something like a `query_to_relay_connection` function you can use like this:

```python
class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    node = relay.Node.Field()

    all_users = graphene.List(User)

    def resolve_all_users(self, info, **args):
        # Grab the root SQLAlchemy query
        query = User.get_query(info)
        query = query.filter(
            User.active,
            # and possibly a whole bunch of other filters)
        ).options(joinedload(User.emails)
        return query_to_relay_connection(query, User)
```

this can probably reuse a lot of code SQLAlchemyConnectionField currently uses internally.

issue_title: Match behaviour of closing LoomConnection with regular file API behaviour
issue_body:
Just a quick check if these API updates are approved before I push them to GitHub:

Normal file connections have a read-only `closed` Bool variable that the user can read to check if the file is closed. Added this to `LoomConnection` too. Basically, `self._closed` is set to false by `__init__`, and to true by `close()`, and accessible through a `closed` getter:

```python
	@property
	def closed(self) -> Bool:
		return self._closed
```

Currently, calling `ds.close()` on an already closed connection raises an error because it calls `self._file.close()` after setting `self._file` to none.

I'm somewhat on the fence on this one, since throwing an error could arguably be considered the right behaviour (telling the programmer to not call `close()` superfluously). 

OTOH, trying to close an already closed connection is harmless, and very annoying if it interrupts a long-running script. Also, calling `f.close()` twice on a regular file does nothing, so that's apparently the Pythonic way that users will expect.

The lack of feedback is a bit stupid IMO, so as a compromise I added a check + a warning for now:

```python
	def close(self) -> None:
		"""
		Close the connection. After this, the connection object becomes invalid.
		"""
		if self._file is None:
			# Warn user that they're being paranoid and should clean up their code
			logging.warn("Connection to %s is already closed", self.filename)
		else:
			self._file.close()
			self._file = None
		self.row_attrs = {}
		self.col_attrs = {}
		self.shape = [0, 0]
		self.closed = true
```

Finally, I wrapped `__init__` in a `try/except` block to ensure we properly close the h5py connection in case it fails on bad input (semi-related to issue #10):

```python
		try:
			# old initialisation code
		except e:
			logging.warn("construction LoomConnection to %s failed, closing file connection", filename)
			self.close()
			raise e
```

issue_title: Test target state event for complete transfer
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

TransferCompleted event must be used only after the secret is learned and there
is enough time to unlock the lock on chain.

A mediated transfer might be received during the settlement period of the
current channel, the secret request is sent to the initiator and at time the
secret is revealed there might not be enough time to safely unlock the token
on-chain.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Define a proper event for the success of the target transfer 
  - [ ] Take into account that mediators also need an event for transfer complete / failed
- [ ] Implement the unit test

issue_title: Make Compatible with macOS
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Reports from users are that Raiden at the moment is [not compatible with macOS](https://twitter.com/homakov/status/908304985892499456). A user in [gitter](https://gitter.im/raiden-network/raiden?at=59b7a9b2bc464729742303a6) reported that there is a dependency problem: 

`pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound: The 'scrypt' distribution was not found and is required by ethereum, pyethapp`

It's quite possible that there are other problems as we have not really tested on Macos. Since it's a popular distro among some devs we should make this a priority.

## Solution

Test raiden on Macos and make sure all functionality works there in the same way as it does in Linux.

## Tasklist

- [x] Test Raiden in macOS and fix all incompatibilities
- [x] Run tests in Travis, or some other CI also for macOS. (Optional - perhaps make another issue for this) will be tracked in #1130
- [ ] Add a changelog entry.

issue_title: Allow to hold off SecretRequest
issue_body:
Following the discussion in #112.

Receiver of a locked (mediated) transfer could be able to hold off asking for the secret, for a particular transfer id.

This is motivated by situations, where the receiver and sender need to agree on some details of the transfer before making it final. At the same time receiver and sender don't want to wait with initialization of the transfer.

This could take the form of a method like `raiden_api.allow_sending_secret_request(id)` and (possibly) providing an option to always allow immediately.

issue_title: TransactionMiddleware not called when exceptions occur during Tastypie processing
issue_body:
Because Tastypie is intercepting exceptions to provide a proper client response, it looks like the TransactionMiddleware is not called properly.  This causes a partial commit of data rather than a rollback.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Ensure 'django.middleware.transaction.TransactionMiddleware' is in your MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES.
2. Samples:

model.py

```
(imports skipped)
class TestModel(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=50, unique=True)
```

---

resource.py

```
(imports skipped)
class TestResource(ModelResource):
    class Meta:
        queryset = TestModel.objects.all()
        resource_name = 'test'
        allowed_methods = ['get','post']

    def obj_create(self, bundle, request=None, **kwargs):
        # Ignore bundle data for this simple test
        testA = TestModel(name='testA')
        testA.save()

        testB = TestModel(name='testA')
        testB.save()  # Should throw db exception and roll everything back

        bundle.obj = testA

        return bundle
```

---

urls.py

```
(imports skipped)
v1_api = Api(api_name='v1')
v1_api.register(TestResource())

urlpatterns = patterns('',
    (r'^api/', include(v1_api.urls)),
)
```
1. Fire up Django and POST to the API:
   
   ```
   curl -sv0 \
   -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
   -X POST \
   -d "{\"name\":\"not used\"}" \
   http://localhost:8000/api/v1/test/ 
   ```
2. Request should error with a unique constraint violation, and the database should be empty.  Currently however, "testA" is created.

issue_title: In SpatialConvolution, weight address changed, but weightMM not
issue_body:
In SpatialConvolution, weightMM is the view of weight and won't be updated until it is empty.

When calling getParameter fuc,  Module.flatten will change the memory address of weight .

So if we call forward/backward and get weight by getParameter, weight's change won't affect nonempty weightMM.

There is a need to change weightMM updated rule.

issue_title: User-selectable preferred output formats
issue_body:
Originally on 2010-09-28

Cf. [[#93691](https://inspirert.cern.ch/Ticket/Display.html?id=93691|rt)].

It would be nice if users could select their preferred output formats and be shown only those options in the display.  So instead of the result list looking like:
```
    - References | BibTeX | LaTeX(US) | LaTeX(EU) | EndNote 
```
they might see (because of the preference they've put into a cookie, or on their user profile page):
```
    - References | LaTeX(US) | EndNote 
```

issue_title: Fail to get flask app context instance inside a celery task
issue_body:
Hi,

I am trying to integrate flask-mail with celery. Flask-mail need a flask application context, I wrote the test code like this:

    from flask import Flask
    from flask.ext.celeryext import FlaskCeleryExt

    app = Flask('example')
    app.config['BROKER_URL'] = 'amqp://guest:guest@127.0.0.1:5672//'
    app.config['CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND'] = None

    ext = FlaskCeleryExt(app)
    celery = ext.celery


    @celery.task(name='test')
    def test():
        from flask import current_app
        assert current_app._get_current_object() is not None

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        result = test.delay()

Then, run the celery worker:

    celery worker -A test.celery --loglevel=info

And test the script:

    python test.py

But it doesn't work. I got an exception: `RuntimeError: working outside of application context`

I google it and fixed it like this:

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        result = test.delay()
    else:
        print('*** push app context ***')
        app.app_context().push()

It works. 

Do I have to push a flask application context manually or misunderstand something ?

Thanks.

issue_title: BaseFeaturizer() doesn't support certain types of featurizers well
issue_body:
Many featurizers don't have a set number or type of features that they return - the features depends on the inut. For example, the BagofBonds featurizers (currently as a PR) will return one value for each potential bond combination in the material. For Li2O this would mean Li-Li, Li-O, and O-O. But for Li (metal) the only feature is Li-Li. Or for Na2S it is Na-Na, Na-S, S-S. So the features are different for each entry. There are multiple featurizers like this, like ChemicalSRO.

There are two issues here:
1. You get this clunky thing where feature_labels() only really works well after you've called featurize(), and is only really pertinent to the most recent featurize() call. This makes the Featurizer a stateful object which is less preferable and more prone to breakage.
2. Sometimes, you need to override featurize_dataframe() to get this to work.

It would be better if someone could design an improvement to BaseFeaturize() to support these kinds of features. I should say that text mining handles this kind of use case all the time. For example, the ``CountVectorizer`` in scikit-learn will return one "feature" for every distinct word in a block of text, and the features will be different for each text block. The way to do this is to use the ``fit_transform()`` method in scikit-learn.

I am wondering if something similar is needed / helpful for some of our featurizers.

issue_title: ModelSchema.load(instance=None) call wont overridden .instance
issue_body:
as in `ModelSchema.load`
```python
    self.instance = instance or self.instance
```
because I instantiation schema class in module level, reuse them across whole project,
the value of `.instance` will persists between `load` calls, this will trigger nasty bug as its value depends on request order.

issue_title: ModelSchema default behaviour needs a lot of overriding
issue_body:
The current behaviour of ModelSchema is not that useful to me. Where I have, say, an "order" many-to-one relationship, by default it dumps a field "order" with the order_id, which does not load. It would be more helpful to dump this as field "order_id". one-to-many relationships are similarly not that useful. 

Right now I am using a lot of excludes and manually defined fields, but this is unnecessary. In fact, marshmallow-sqlalchemy already has much more helpful default logic, on TableSchema, especially with include_fk=True. I can't directly use TableSchema as it only supports dump and I also need to load.

However, a one line change to schema.py really help:

```
return converter.fields_for_model(opts.model, fields=opts.fields, exclude=opts.exclude)
-->
return converter.fields_for_table(opts.model.__table__, include_fk=True, fields=opts.fields, exclude=opts.exclude)
```

I realise this would break compatibility, but it seems a much more helpful default to me.

issue_title: Pysam fails to compile under Python 3.4
issue_body:
Hello pysam devs,

I have a problem trying to get pysam running in my environment. I'm on Ubuntu 12.04 running Python 3.4.2 (with virtualenv) trying to install pysam 0.8.2.1 from pip. I've also installed Cython 0.20.2 (per pysam's docs).

Everytime I try to install pysam, I get a long stack trace which ends in this:

```
...

    pysam/chtslib.pxd:102:27: 'off_t' is not a type identifier
    
    
    
    Error compiling Cython file:
    
    ------------------------------------------------------------
    
    ...
    
        #   or negative if an error occurred.
    
        off_t hseek(hFILE *fp, off_t offset, int whence)
    
    
    
        # @abstract  Report the current stream offset
    
        # @return    The offset within the stream, starting from zero.
    
        off_t htell(hFILE *fp)
    
       ^
    
    ------------------------------------------------------------
    
    
    
    pysam/chtslib.pxd:106:4: 'off_t' is not a type identifier
    
    error: command 'x86_64-linux-gnu-gcc' failed with exit status 1
    
    ----------------------------------------
    Command "/home/warindrarto/.virtualenvs/testing3/bin/python3.4 -c "import setuptools, tokenize;__file__='/tmp/pip-build-n4lcer4e/pysam/setup.py';exec(compile(getattr(tokenize, 'open', open)(__file__).read().replace('\r\n', '\n'), __file__, 'exec'))" install --record /tmp/pip-ec_gyp1a-record/install-record.txt --single-version-externally-managed --compile --install-headers /home/warindrarto/.virtualenvs/testing3/include/site/python3.4" failed with error code 1 in /tmp/pip-build-n4lcer4e/pysam
```

Has anyone encountered this issue or does anyone have any pointers on how to solve this? Please let me know if the full stack trace could be useful as well (so I can put them somewhere online).

I am working on a CentOS 6.8 cluster where I don't have root access.
This system uses the modules system to load software:
http://modules.sourceforge.net/

To make things a bit neater and easier on myself, I use virtualenvs to install python stuff. This system has python 2.6 running natively and other pythons are loaded via the module system. Thus, I load python3 before creating a new virtualenv for ariba. Thus my python version is now

issue_title: remove django_extensions/management/utils:get_project_root
issue_body:
Remove the ```get_project_root``` method for version 1.4.0

New Django installs specify BASE_DIR in settings.py which can be used instead for a consistent way to get the project root.

We should promote users adding BASE_DIR and explicitly telling us the project root.

issue_title: Recurrence.between() doesn't exclude Feb 29th when Recurrence has empty dtstart and inclusion is Daily
issue_body:
The following failing test describes the issue:

https://github.com/django-recurrence/django-recurrence/compare/master...alextreme:feb29-exclusion?expand=1

It's an edge-case that only occurs with between() when the Recurrence is daily and the exclusion is Feb 29th and dtstart isn't filled in.

I've been able to easily work around it by either setting dtstart or providing an alternative exclusion rule, but I'm noting this issue in case anyone else stumbles upon it (today or over 4 years).

issue_title: Not possible to set the datastore namespace globally.

The last example for this method has a snippet:

```python
>>> from gcloud import datastore

>>> with Batch() as batch:
```

issue_body:
See: https://circleci.com/gh/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/7040

```python
____________ TestBigQuery.test_insert_rows_nested_nested_dictionary ____________

self = <tests.system.TestBigQuery testMethod=test_insert_rows_nested_nested_dictionary>

    def test_insert_rows_nested_nested_dictionary(self):
        # See #2951
        SF = bigquery.SchemaField
        schema = [
            SF('string_col', 'STRING', mode='NULLABLE'),
            SF('record_col', 'RECORD', mode='NULLABLE', fields=[
                SF('nested_string', 'STRING', mode='NULLABLE'),
                SF('nested_repeated', 'INTEGER', mode='REPEATED'),
                SF('nested_record', 'RECORD', mode='NULLABLE', fields=[
                    SF('nested_nested_string', 'STRING', mode='NULLABLE'),
                ]),
            ]),
        ]
        record = {
            'nested_string': 'another string value',
            'nested_repeated': [0, 1, 2],
            'nested_record': {'nested_nested_string': 'some deep insight'},
        }
        to_insert = [
            {'string_col': 'Some value', 'record_col': record}
        ]
```

issue_title: Monitoring TimeSeries.Query docs: broken links
issue_body:
Today (9 March), I received a notice from a link checker that the file at [http://gcloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/monitoring/query.html](http://gcloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/monitoring/query.html) had two bad links to the Stackdriver Monitoring API reference.

Here are two warnings I found:

W,https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/api/ref_v3/rest/v3/projects.timeSeries/list#Aligner,http://gcloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/monitoring/query.html,Anchor: Aligner not found.
W,https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/api/ref_v3/rest/v3/projects.timeSeries/list#Reducer,http://gcloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/monitoring/query.html,Anchor: Reducer not found.

The Aligner and Reducer tags are no longer in the monitoring/api/ref_v3/rest/v3/projects.timeSeries/list.html file. This is a generated file, and in the most recent regeneration of the API (published on 8 March), these two tags and the content associated with them was moved (by the generating tool) to monitoring/api/ref_v3/rest/v3/projects.alertPolicies.html. 
Here's the [alertPolicies](https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/api/ref_v3/rest/v3/projects.alertPolicies) page.

I apologize for this. I was able to pre-emptively fix the links in my own docs that this change would break, but I had no way to know from where else these anchors might be linked to. And I cannot "fix" this with the usual URL redirects mechanism we use when pages move. This is content in a page.

I have filed a bug against the generator for this behavior.

issue_title: pubsub: streaming pull needs keepalive
issue_body: With default configuration, streaming pull will hang if the stream is idle for a few minutes. This usually happens when the subscription has no more messages. In Go, we set gRPC keepalive to 5 minutes [(CL)](https://code-review.googlesource.com/#/c/gocloud/+/17070/). A similar option should be available in Python, though I'm not sure what the code to pass in the option looks like.

issue_title: Kill node.js process when cwltool exits
issue_body: At the moment the node.js process spawned by cwltool for executing JavaScript expressions and validation of JavaScript doesn't get killed when cwltool exits. It would be nice if this happened.

issue_title: Enable reverse lookup of files when returning JSON
issue_body:
This summarizes a local discussion with @tetron.

Currently when running a CWL workflow inside a Docker container, there is no reverse mapping of outputs back to the original file locations.

When you are re-shaping input this results in not being able to find returned output. For example, we have code that re-groups BAM files into batches for simultaneous variant calling but returning regrouped JSON with a path to the original file (inside the Docker container) results in errors downstream since the file is not remapped externally.

The implementation fix is to add reverse lookups to [collect_output_ports](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/blob/f0487153700af34f34d1344d88df5e88b853eb4c/cwltool/draft2tool.py#L184). This requires adding a `reversemap` to [DockerPathMapper](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/blob/f0487153700af34f34d1344d88df5e88b853eb4c/cwltool/pathmapper.py#L36) and using it to remap with [adjustFiles](https://github.com/common-workflow-language/cwltool/blob/f0487153700af34f34d1344d88df5e88b853eb4c/cwltool/process.py#L132).

issue_title: Using django-filters with this module
issue_body:
Has anyone been able to make this app work together with django-filter? I have tried but for some reason at the last minute my queryset gets overriden this is because all the parameters for the datatable get passed via the request.GET which is what django-filters use:

```py3
from datatableview import Datatable
from datatableview.views import DatatableView
from django.db import models
from django_filters import FilterSet
from django_filters.views import FilterMixin


class Student(models.Model):
    school = models.CharField(max_length=255, blank=True, null=True)
    grade = models.CharField(max_length=255, blank=True, null=True)
    last_name = models.CharField(max_length=255, blank=True, null=True)
    first_name = models.CharField(max_length=255, blank=True, null=True)
    middle_name = models.CharField(max_length=255, blank=True, null=True)


class StudentFilter(FilterSet):
    class Meta:
        model = Student
        fields = ['school', 'grade']


class StudentDataTable(Datatable):
    class Meta:
        model = Student
        structure_template = 'datatableview/bootstrap_structure.html'
        columns = ['school', 'grade', 'last_name', 'first_name']


class StudentListView(FilterMixin, DatatableView):
    model = Student
    datatable_class = StudentDataTable
    template_name = 'afb/datatable.html'
    filter = None

    def get_queryset(self):
        qs = super(StudentListView, self).get_queryset()
        self.filter = StudentFilter(self.request.GET, queryset=qs)
        return self.filter.qs

    def get_context_data(self, **kwargs):
        context = super(StudentListView, self).get_context_data(**kwargs)
        context['filter'] = self.filter
        return context
```
